To starve patients was never my plan ; but I am clearly of opinion, that in the use of all the purging mineral waters, a light and rather diluting diet is the most proper; and thai no person, during such a course, ought to eat to the full extent of what his appetite craves. To promote the operation of mineral waters, and to carry them throiit;li the system, exercise is iudispen-ably necessaiy. This may be taken in any manner that is most agreeable to the patient; blithe ought never to carry it to excess. The best kinds of exercise are those connected with amusement. Every thing that tends to exhilarate the 'spirit, not only promotes the operation of the waters, but acts as a medicine. All wdio resort to the mineral waters ought therefore to leave every care, behind, to mix with the company and to make them- selves as cheerful and hippy as possible. From this conduct, assisted by the free and wholesome airof those fashionable places of resort, and also the regular and early hours which are usually kept, the patient often receiv es more benefi t than from using the waters. \ But the greatest errors in drinl.in^ the purging mineral waters arise from their being used in cases v. here iln-y are absolutely improper, and adverse to the liati.re of the disease. \. ben people hear ofa wonder, ul cure having been performed by some mineral water, they immediately conclude tli.it it will cure every thing, aud accordingly swallow it down, w hen tltey migh. as well take poison. Patients ought to be well informed, before they begin to drink the more actire kinds of mineral waters, of the propriety of the course, and should never persist in using tlieni when they are found to aggravate the disorder. In all cases where purging is indicated, the saline mineral waters will be found to fulfil this intention better than any other medicine. • When I speak of drinking a glass of the water over-night, I must beg leave to can- tin those wlm fallow this plan agiumt eating heavy suppers. Ihe late Dr. Daultiy or Vmk who was the first that brought the Harrowgate waters into repute, used to advise lii> patients to drink a glass before-the) went to bed; the consequence of which was, that bavin- eat a flesh supper, and the water operating in tbe night, theywereotten win uted »itb grilles and obliged to call for medical assistance. 370 OBSERVATIONS CONCERNING DIET, Sec Their operation, if taken in proper quantity, is generally mild; and they are neither found to irritate the nerves, uor debilitate the patient so much as the other purgatives. As a purgative, these waters are chiefly recommended in diseases of the first passages,accompanied with, or proceetling from,inactivity of tbe stomach and bowels, acidity, indigestion, vitiated bile, worms, pu- trid sordes, the piles, and jaundice. In most cases of this kind they are the best medicines that can be administered. But when used with tins view, it is sufficient to take them twice, or at most three times a-week, so as to move the body three or four times; and it will be proper to continue this course for some weeks. But the operation of the more active mineral waters is not confined to the bowels. They often promote the discharge of urine, and not un- frequently increase the perspiration. This shews that they are capable of penetrating into every part of the body, and of stimulating the whole system. Hence arises their efficacy in removing the most obstinate of all disorders, obstructions of the glandular and lymphatic system. Under this class is comprehended the scrophula or King's-evil, indolent tu- mours, obstructions of the liver, spleeu, kidnies, and mesenteric glands. When these great purposes are to be effected, the wateis must be used in the gradual manner mentioned above, and persisted in for a length of time. It will be proper however now and then to discontinue tlieir use for a few days. Tlie next great class of diseases where mineral waters arc found to be beneficial, are those of the skin, as the itch, scab, tetters, ring- worms, scaly eruptions, leprosies, blotches, foul ulcer?, &c. Though these may setm superficial, yet they are often the most obstinate which the physician has to encounter, and not {infrequently set his skill at defiance: but they jjrill sometimes yield to the application of mineral wateis for a sufficient length of time, and in mo-i cases of this kind these waters deserve a trial. The saline sulphureous waters, such as those of Moffat in Scotland, and Harrowgate in England, are the roost likely to succeed in diseases ofthe skin; but for this purpose it will be necessary not only to drink the waters, but likewise to use them externally. To enumerate more particularly the qualities ofthe different mineral waters, to specify those diseases in which they are respectivtly indica- ted, and to point out their proper modes of application, would bean useful, and by no means a disagreeable employment; but as the limits prescribed to these remarks will not allow me to treat the subject at more length, I shall conclude by observing, that whenever the mineral waters are found to exhaust the strength, depress the spirits, take away the appetite, excite fevers, distend the bowels, or occasion a cough, they ought to be discontinued. CHAPTER LVIII. Obserrations concerning the DIET OF Till-: COMMON PEOPLE, recommending a mithodof living less expensive, and more conducive to health, than the preset, t. EXPERIENCE proves that not a few ofthe diseases incident to the inhabitants of this country, are owing to tlieir mode of living. The vegetable productions they consume, fall considerably short of the pro- portion wliich they ought to bear to the animal part of their food. The constant use of bread aud animal substances excites an unnatural GENERAL OBSERVATIONS ON ALIMENT. 371 thirst, and leads to the immoderate use of beer and other stimulating liquors, which generate disease and reduce the lower orders of people to a state of indigence. To teach the poor man how to live cheaper and better, is the design of tbe following pages. Though the common people of this country live at a greater expense than any where else, it does not follow that they live better. They are strong indeed, but by no means healthy; and it is found that, from an attachment to a particular mode of living, they are more liable to disease and death in foreign climes, than the inhabitants of any other country. It is certainly proper that the poor man should be instructed in every thing that can make his little earnings go as far as possible, or which can add to the comfort of himself and family. Nor can oeconomy in living, be deemed trivial, in a country where the riches depend on the cheapness of labour. It is alleged that the English are so much attached to their own modes of living, that no argument will induce them to make the small- est change. Habits are indeed obstinate things, especially those which relate to diet; but tliere are proofs that the English are not inflexible even in this matter. The mode of living among the lower orders has been greatly changed in my time, and I am sorry to say, not for the better. The people of England have too much good sense not to listen to i reason provided due care were taken to instruct them. But here the | people may be truly said " to perish for want of knowledge." No means have been used to give them proper instruction. Hurtful customs have been suffered to prevail, till they have struck such deep roots that it will not be an easy matter to eradicate them. The difficulty, however, is not {insurmountable. A few experiments of reform would have the effect to render it as agreeable as it is salutary. Adults have many old prejudices to overcome, but the case is differ- ent in regard to children. They may be taught to use any kind of food, i^ and what they use when young they will love when old. If I can intro- ; doce a different method of feeding children, my purposes will be an- swered. This alone will, in time, effect a total change in the general mode of living. The late distress ofthe poor has called forth many publications in- tended for their relief. Most of them, however, were adapted only for the particular occasion, and not calculated to prevent the return of like evils. The following observations, it is hoped, will have a more permanent effect. They are intended to recommend a plan of living, which will render the people less dependent on bread and animal food for their subsistence, and consequently not so liable to suffer from a scarcity or dearth of either of these articles in future. Particular attention has been paid to the substitutes for bread, as the scarcity of this article proves peculiarly distressing to the poor. It will appear from the following pages, that bread is by no means so much a necessary of life as generally imagined, and that its place may, I in many instances, be supplied by a variety of other farinaceous sub- stances. GENERAL OBSERVATIONS ON ALIMENT. NO creature eats snch a variety of food as man. Intended for an in- habitant of every climate, he devours Hie productions of them all; and if they do not suit his palate, or agree with his stomach, he calls in the aid of cookery, an art peculiar to himself; by which many things that, 372 GENERAL OBSERVATIONS ON ALIMENT. in a crude state, would prove hurtful, or even poisonous, are rendered wholesome and salutary. The obvious division of food is into animal and vegetable. To sa\ that man was intended by nature for using either the one or the other alone, would be absurd. His structure and appetite prove that he was formed for both. Judgment,however, i- requisite in adjusting the proportions of each, so as to avoid the incouveniencies, arising from an extreme on either hand. Though animal food is more nourishing than vegetable, it is not safe to live on that alone. Experience has shewn that a diet consistiag solely of animal food, excites thirst, and nausea, occasions putrescence ( in the stomach and bowels, and finally brings on violent griping pains ( with cholera and dysentery. Animal food is less adapted to the sedentary than the laborious, anil least of all to the studious, whose diet ought to consist chiefly of vegeta- bles. Indulging in animal food renders men dull, and unfit for the pursuits of science, especially when itis accompanied with the free use of strong liquors. The plethoric, or person j ofa full habit, should eat sparingly of ani- mal food- It yields far more blood than vegetables taken in the-.unit , - quantity, and of course may induce inflammatory disorders. It acts J as a stimulus to the whole system, by which means the circulation of the blood is greatly accelerated. ' I am inclined to think, that consumptions, so common in England, are in part owing to the great use of animal food. Though the I'hthis- i ■is Pubnovalis properly spiaking, is not an inflammatory disease, yet it generally begins with symptoms of inflammation, and is often accompa- nied with them through its whole progress. But the disease most common to this country is the scurvy. One finds a dash ofit in almost every family, and in some the taint is very deep. A disease so general must have a g* neral cause, and there is none so obvious as the great quantity of animal food devoured by tue natives. As a proof that scurvy arises from this cause, we are m pos- session of no reaiedy for that disease equal to the free use of fresh ve- getables. By the uninterrupted use of animal food a putrid diathesis is induced in the system, wliich predisposes to a variety of disorders. I am fully convinced that many of those obstiru e complaint* for vhich v e areat aloss to account, and find it still more difficult to cure, are the effects of a scorbutic taint lurking in the habit. Improper diet affects the mind as well as the body. The choleric disposition of the English is almost proverbial. Were I to a-si/rn a cause, it would be, their living so much on animal food. Thin- is no doubt but this induces a ferocity of temper unknown to men whose food is chiefly taken from the vegetable kingdom. Though these ^nd similar consequences may arise from the excess ofanimal diet, we are far from discouragin* its use in moderation. In all cold countries it is certainly necessary ; but the major part ofthe aliment ought nevertheless to consist of vegetable substances. There is a continual tendency of animal food, as well as in the human body itself, to putrefaction, which can only be counteiacted by the free use of vegetables. With regard to the proportion of vegetable foot! to that of animal, great nicety is by no means required. It mus'. vary according to cir- cumstances, as the heat of the weather, the warmth ofthe climate, OF BREAD. 373 and the like. The vegetable part, however, where nothing forbids, ought certainly to preponderate, and I think in the proportion of at least two to one. The excessive consumption of animal food is one great cause ofthe Bcarcity of jrr-din. The food that a bullock affords bears but a small pro- portion to the quantity of vegetable, matter he consumes. I am no enemy to good fruit, as an article of diet ; but the greater part of what is used in this country, by the lower orders ofthe people, is mere trash. Fruit should be eaten in the early part of the day when the stomach is not loaded with food, and it never ought to be eaten raw till it be thoroughly ripe. OF BREAD. BREAD, or something resembling it, makes a part of the diet of all nations. Hence it is emphatically denominated the stuff of life. It may, however, be used too freely. The late Dr. Fotliergill was of opinion, ami I perfectly airree with him, that most people eat more bread than is conducive to tlieir health. 1 do not mean to insinuate that bread is unwholesome, but that the best things may prove hurtful when taken to excess. A surfeit of bread is more dangerous than of any other food. Omnis repletio mala repletio panis pessima. The French consume vast quantities of bread ; but its bad effects are prevented by their copious use of soups and fruits, which have little or no share in the diet ofthe common people of England. One important use of bread is to form a mass fit for filling up the ali- mentary canal, and carrying the nutritious juices along that passage in such a state, as to render them fit to be acted upon by the lacteal absor. bents, which take up the nourishment and convey it to the blood. In thishftlit bread may be considered as a soil from whence the nourish- ment is drawn. I do not say that bread contains no nourishment, but that its use, as an article of diet, does not solely depend on the quan- tity of nutriment it contains, but in some measure on its fitness as a ve- hicle for conveying the nutritious particles through Ihe intestinal tubes. Hence it follows, that the finest bread it not always the best adapted for answering the purposes of nutrition. The richest food will not nourish an animal, unless the alimentary ca« nal is sufficiently distended. A dog has been fed on the richest broth yet could not be kept alive; while another, which had only the meat boiled to chip and water, throve very well. This shews the folly of at- tempting to nourish men on alimentary powders and other concentra- ted food. The great art therefore of preparing food, is to blend the nutritive part ofthe aliment with a sufficient quantity of some light farinaceous mbstance, in order to fill up the canal, witliout overcharging it with more nutritious particles than are necessary for the support ofthe ani- mal. This may be done either by bread, or other farinaceous sub- stances, of which tliere is a great variety, as will appear from the sequel. Bread is one ofthe most expensive modes of using grain, and not adapted to the narrow' cii enmstunces of the lower orders of the peo- ple, as it is bin thened with two heavy additional charges,in passing tluou<>h the hands of both the miller and the baker Besides, the for* mcr often grinds down extraneous matter with the wheat, and the latter as frequently bakes it up with the addition of lime, chalk, alum, and oilier pernicious substances. Since the articles of diet have becoma branches of manufacture, the public neither know what they eat, nor what they drink. •4 OF BREAD. People imagine, as the finest flour contains the greatest spinntity of nourishment, that it must therefore be the most proper for making into bread ; but this by no means follow s. The finest flour comes the near- est tostarch, wliich, though it may occasionally prove a ijood medicine, makes bad bread. Household bread, which is made by grinding down the whole grain, and only separating the coarser bran, is without doubt the most wholesome. The best household bread I ever remember to have eat, was in the county of York. It was what they call meslin bread, and consisted of wheat anil rye ground together. I am not quite certain as to the pro- portion ; but I think tliere might be two parts of the former to one of the latter. This bread, when well fermented, eats light, isuf a pleasant taste, and soluble to the bowels. After using it tor some years, I found that bread made entirely of flour was neither so agreeable to the palate, nor so conducive to health. Bread is often spoiled to please the eye. The artificially whitened, drying, stuffing bread, though made ofthe heart ofthe wheat, is in real- ity the worst of any ; yet this is the bread which most people prefer, and the poorer sort will cat no other. All the different kinds of grain are occa^pnally made into bread, some giving preference to one and seme to another, according to early cus- tom aad prejudice. The people of South Britain generally prefer bread made of the finest wheat flour, while those of the northern counties eat a mixture of flour and oatmeal, or ryemeal, and many give the pre- ference to bread made of oatmeal alone. The common people of Scot- land also eat a mixed bread, but more frequently bread of oatmeal on- ly. In Germany the common bread is made of rye, and the American labourer thinks no bread so strengthening as that wliich is made of In- dian corn; nor do I much doubt but the Laplander thinks his bread, made ofthe bones of fishes, is the best of any. Bread made of different kinds of grain is more wholesome than what is made of one only, as t!u ir qualities serve to correct one another. For example, wheat Hour, especially the finer kind, being of a starchy nature, is apt to occasion constipation. Bread made of ryemeal, on the other hand, proves often too slippery for the bowels. A due pro- portion of these makes the best bread. For the more active and laborious I would recommend a mixture of rye with the stronger grains, as peas, beans, barley, oat«, Indian corn, and the like. These maybe blended in many different ways; they make a hearty bread for a labouring man, and to us« his own language, they lie longer, on his stomach than bread made of wheat flour only. B.uh-y bread passes too quickly through the alimentary canal to afford time for conveying the proper nourishment; but bread made of bar'ey mixed with pease is very nourishing. When potatoes, or boiled grain, arc, used, bread ceases to be a neces- rary article of diet. During the late scarcity of bread, 1 made it a rule not to eat above one half the quantity I used to do, and I found no mconveuiency whatever from the change. Nay, some told me, that for a considerable lime they had left off the use of bread altogether, without experiencing any change in the stiteof their health. A great part of the bread consumed in this country is by children. It is alwavs ready, and when the child calls for food, a piece of bread is >iii into its hand, to save the trouble of dressing any other kind of victuals. Of many children this is the principal food, but it i- f*r from hems; the most proper. Children are often troubled with acidities of (he stomach and bowels; and it is well known that bread mixed with OF BREAD. 375 water, and kept in a degree, of heat equal to that of tie human stom- ach, soon turns sour. During the late scarcity, many of the labouring men, and even arti- ficers, could not earn as much money as was sufficient to keep their families in the article of bread only. It is certain, however, that on a different plan, such familic- might have lived very comfortably. Many of the articles of diet are cheaper than bread, and equally wholesome. Above one half of the expense of living might be saved by a due selec- tion of the. article of diet. The English labourer lives chiefly on bread, which being ac- companied with other dry, and often salt food, fires his blood, and excites an unquenchable thirst, so that the. perpetual cry is ftr drink. But the greatest consumption of bread is occasioned by tea. Itis said that the subjects of Great Britain consume a greater quantity of that herb, than the whole inhabitants of all the other nations of this quarter of the globe. Tbe poorest woman in England must have her tea, and the children generally share it with bcr. As tea contains no nourishment either for young or old, there must of course be bread and butter to eat along with it. The quartern loaf will not go far among a family of hungry children, and if we add the cost of tea, sugar, butter, and milk, the expense of one meal will be more than would be sufficient to till their bellies with wholesome food three times a-dav. There is reason to believe that one half the bread consumed in Eng- land is used to tea, without one hearty meal ever being made ofit. The higher ranks use tea as a luxury, while the lower order-- make a dint of it. I had lately occasion to see a striking instance of this in a family that was represented to me as in distress for want of bread. I sent them a little money, and was informed that they ran with it directly to the tea-shop. To a heavy, sluggish, phlegmatic man, a moderate use of tea may not prove pernicious ; but vvliere there is a debilitated stomach and an irritability of fibre, it m:ver fails to do much hurt. With many it has the effect to 'invent sleep. Tea will produce a total change of constitution in the people of this country. Indeed it has gone a great way towards effecting thai evil already. A debility, and 'consequent irntai.ihly of iinrc, are become so common, that not only women, but even men ai e affected with them. That class of diseases, which, for want?of a better name, we call ner- vous, has made almost a complete conquest of the one sex, and is ma- king hasty strides towards vanquishing the other. Did women know the train of dheases induced by debility, and bow disagreeable tbi-se oi-cases under them to the oilier sex, they would shun tea as the most deadiy poison. No man can love a woman eaten up with viqionrs, or washed lown with diseases arising fiom relax- ation. It is not tea taking as a beverage after a full meal, or in a crowded assembly, that I so much condemn, though I think something as elegant and kss pernicious inn-ht be substituted in i'.* place. Vim mischief oc- casioncd bv tea, ari eschictlv from its being substituted for solid food. This is so much the case at present, that, bad I time to spare, I think it could not be better employed than in wnti:■? ajjainst t!^dcM>-u five J rug 37d OF BOILED GRAIN. THOUGH farinaceous substances, of one kind or another, make a necessary part of the food of man, yet there can be no reason why snch substances should alway s assume the name and form of bread. Many of them are more wholesome, and not less agreeable in other forms. Ui<\ul is often used merely to save the trouble of cookery : and being portable, is the most convenient article of diet for carrying ;;b road. It docs not, however, admit of a doubt, that more grain is eaten boiled, though not in this country, than is made into bread; aud that this mode of cookery is the most wholesome. Simple boiling precludes all adulteration, and is an operation much less laborious and artificial than baking. The most general article of diet among mankind, is rice. This may be made into a variety of dishes ; but simple boiling is all that is re- quired to rentier it a proper substitute for bread. It may either be eaten alone or w itli milk. In the east, it is u-cd with meat, in the same manner as we do bread. The people of this country believe that rice provesinjurious to the eyes, but this seems to be without foundation ; as it has no such effect on tho>e who make it the principal part of their food. Many other kinds of grain will, when boiled, make good substitutes for bread. Even those which make a harsh anil unpleasant sort of bread, are. often rendered very palatable by boiling. This is the case with all the leguminous class of plants, as peas, beans, Sec. Even oats and barley are more agreeable, as well as more wholesome, when boil- ed, than made into bread. Al! allow that pease and beans boiled, when young, are a great luxury. But when old, they are equally wholesome, and, when properly cook- ed, by no means unpleasant. Tliere are few who do no relish pea e pud- ding, and even prefer it to bread. Beans are not so fit for this pur- pose ; but they make an excellent ingredient in the poor man's bioth, and whoever eats this broth, will find iittie occasion for bread. Pea e and brans contain an equal quantity of sugar with wheat, oats, or barley, and at the same time a greater proportion of oil; consequent- ly are more nourishing. This fact is confirmed by daily experience. On those farms where pi as aud beans are raised in gnat abundance, the labourers are much ted on that sort of grain; but when removed to farms where they are fed with other kinds of grain, tltcy soon com- plain ofa diminution of strength, and request a supply of pease meal ai formerly. Nature seems to have pointed out the propriety ofthe extensive use of peas and beans, it being a fact, that when crops of that hind are duly alternated with crops of wheat, bar.ey, or oats, the feitility ofthe soil may be maintained, without rest or manure, for many years together; whereas, if the latter be raised, on the same soil for several years suc- cessively, they render it barren, so that, without rest or manure, its fer- tility cannot be preserved. The people in England are but little accustomed to the use of boiled grain, though in many countries it is eaten as a luxury. Boiled bailey is a great favourite with the Dutch, and is eaten with milk, butter, or molasses. It is the principalfood of the Dutch sailors, who, in gcneiV, are both healthy and robust." Barley is one of the best ingredients in soup. Count Rumford says, it possesses the quality of lulling, or thickening soups, in a. OF BOILED GRAIN. 377 superior degree to any other grain. We have reason, however, to believe, that grits, or coarse oatmeal, will answer that purpose still better. Oatmeal is frequently made into bread; but it is a much more whole- some, as well as agreeable food, when made into hasty pudding, and eaten with milk. The peasants in many parts of Britain make two meals a day ofit, while their children almost wholly mbsist on it, and, it is well know that both old and young who are thus fed, are healthy and robust. The opinion of oatmeal being heating, and occasioning skin diseases, is wholly without foundation. Bread made of oatmeal, when not leav- ened, will sometimes occasion the heart-burn; but this is no proof of its heating quality. Unleavened bread, of wheat or any other grain, produces the same effect on a debilitated stomach. Oatmeal thorough- ly boiled seldom gives the heart-burn. Persons who are fed on oatmeal bread, or hasty pudding, are ivit. more subject to diseases ofthe skin, than those who live on wheat meal. Cutaneous disorders proceed more from the want of clcanlinc-s, than from any particular aliment. The French, so far from thinking that oatmeal is heating, speak ofit as possessed of a cooling quality; and even the English give oatmeal, or gritguiel, to lying-in women, and sick people of every description, which shews that they are inconsistent with themselves, in alleging that the blood is tired by tho use of oatmeal. A lieutenant of the army, residing at a country village within a few miles of Edinburgh, with a wife and ten children, having no other in- come than his half pay, fed the whole of his children with hasty pud- ding and butter-milk only, from a conviction that ii was the most wholesome and full diet, that fell withu the reach of bis narrow cir- cumstances. They grew apace, and it was the universal remark of the neighbourhood, that they were as sprightly, healthy, and robust as other children, and at the same time perfectly free from all skin di- seases. Children are seldom well, unless when their bodies are gently open. But this is more likely to be tho case when fed on oatmeal and milk, than when their bellies are crammed with a starchy substance nvide of the finest flour; yet this in England is the common food of children. I have seen an infant stuffed four or live times a-day with this kind of food. There needs no conjurer to tell the consequence, A late Author, a man of learning but the dnpe of prejudice, has, by a ridiculous definition, endeavoured to represent oats as proper food for horses only. I wish the hoi es in England devoured a smaller quan- tity of that grain, and tlie people more. Few things Wutild have a greater tendency to lessen the expense of living. The oats in Noith Britain are of a superior quality, and I hope the people w ill long have the sense to use them as an article o>' diet. Indian corn is likewise said to make the- best fjod when boiled. fount Rumford observes, that of all things it makes the best puddu g, and that he ha- made a hearty meal ofit, sauce included, for live farth- ings. What makes good puddings will make good dumplings, and there will, at any time, supply the place of bread. The Count aNo re- * marks, that the negroes 111 Amenca prefer Indian corn to rice, and that the Bavarian peasants prefer it to wheal; that it might beirnv.-rted from North An.eiica at about four or live sliillit us • ..-busks 1; that, when made into flour, it would tost only one penny farthing per pound; and that it N highlv nutritious, ami the cheapest toed known. !');,,■ " w " lis 578 OF BUTTER. lag the late scarcity a large quantity of this graiu was imported ; but such is the aversion of the common people of this country to every sort of food to wliich they are not accustomed, that they refustid to purchase it, and the merchants were very great losers by the impor- tation. On the same principle the Germans, till within these few years, could not be induced to eat potatoes, though now they have become extremely fond of them. The American, the. Italian, and the German, all cook Indian corn, in the same, way that the North Britain does his oatmeal, by making it into hasty pudding. It may be eaten in a variety of ways. Some eat it with a sauce composed of butter and brown sugar, or butter anil mo- lasses. Others eat it with milk only. In either way it makes a good, cheap and wholesome diet, by no means disagreeable to those who are accustomed to it. The only other grain we shall mention as best when boiled, is buck- wheat : It is ofa very mucilaginous nature, and of course highly nutri- tious. In several parts of Europe, it constitutes a principal part of tbe food of the lower people. In former times it was eaten in Russia, not by the lower classes only, even the nobility made use ofit. Boiled and buttered, it was so great a favourite of the great Czar Peter, that he is said to have seldom supped on any thing tie. OF BUTTER. IT has been said that the English have a thousand religions and but one sauce. It must be allowed that they use butter with almost every kind of food. Butter, though a good article of diet, may be used too freely, and in this country, I am convinced, that is the case. To weak stomachs it is hurtful, even in small quantities, and when used freely, it proves prejudicial to the strongest. Butter, like other things of an oily nature, has a constant tendency to turn rancid. This process, hy the heat ofthe stomach, is greatly accel- erated, insomuch that many people, soon after eating butter, complain of its rising in their stomach, in a state highly disagreeable Oils of every kind are with difficulty mixed with w atery fluids. Tins is the reason why butter floats on the stomach, and rises in such an uup.ea-ant manner. Person- afflicted with bile should use butter very sparingly. Some sctptical authors doubt whether or not aliment of any kind has an ef- fect on the bile. One thing, however, is certain, that many patients, afflicted with complaints, which were supposed to be occasioned by bile, have been completely cured by a total abstinence from butter. The most violent bilious complaints that I ever met with, were evi- dently occasioned by food that became rancid on the stomach, as the cliolera morbus and the like. Nor can such complaint:, be cured, till tne rancid matter is totally evacuated by vomiting and purging. But supposing butter did not possess the quality of becoming rancid ou the stomach, it may uevertlieles.s, prove hurtful to digestion. Oils of all kinds are ofa relaxing quality, and teud to impede the action of di- gestion. Hence tbe custom of giving rich broths aud fat meats to per- sons who have a voracious appetite. The free use of butter, and other oily substances, not only tends to relax the stomach, and impede its action, but to induce a debility of the solids, which paves the way to many maladies. In a country where two thirds of the inhabitants lead sedentary lives, a debility of fibre roust predominate. Whatever increases that debility ought to be avoided. OF BUTTER. 379 Children, without exception, are disposed to diseases arising from re- laxation. Butter, of course, ought to be given to thein with a sparing hand. But is this the case ? By no means. Bread and butter constitute a great part ofthe food of children, and I am convinced that the gross humours with which they are frequently troubled, are partly owing to this food. As children abound with moisture, bread alone is, generally speaking, better for them than bread and butter. I have been astonished to see the quantities of butter eaten by gross women who lead sedentary lives. Their tea bread is generally con- trived so as to suck up butter like a sponge. What quantities of crumpets and muffins they will devour in a morning, soaked with tliia oil; and afterwards complain of indigestion, when they have eaten what would overload the stomach ofa ploughman. Dr. Fotliergill is of opinion, that butter produces the nervous or sick head-ach, so common among the women of this country. Asa proof of this, it is often cured ' by an emetic. Oils, in certain quantities, excite nausea, and even vomiting. They most of course prove unfriendly to digestion. A Dutch sailor, we are told, can digest train oil. So may an English sailor; but it would be very improper food for a London lady. To some ofthe leaner farinaceous substances, as the potatoe and the like, butter makes a very proper addition; but eating it to flesh and fish of almost every description, is certainly wrong. The flesh eaten in this country is generally fat enough without the addition of butter, and the more oily kinds offish, as salmon or herrings, are lighter on the I stomach, and easier digested when eaten without it -• Butter is rather a gross food, and fitter for the athletic and labo- rious, than the sedentary and delicate. It is less hurtful when eaten ] fresh than suited. Salt butter certainly tends to induce skin diseases, and I am inclined to think, the free use ofit at sea may have some share in bringing ou that dreadful malady, so destructive to our brave sailors, the sea ticurty. There is a method of rendering salt butter less hurtful, but it seems not to be known in England. What I mean is to mix it with an equal quantity of honey, and keep it for use. In this way it may be given to children with greater freedom. In North Britain this method of mixing butter with honey is well known, and from a common proverb, I take the custom to be very ancient. Butter, in itself, is not near so hurtful, as when combined with certain other things. For example: bread made with butler is almost • indigestible, and pastries of every kind are little better: yet many people almost live upon pastry, and it is universally given to children. It is little better, however, than poison, and never fails to disorder tlieir stomachs. The fond mother cannot pass a pastry shop, without 1 treating her darling boy with some of the dainties, and then wonders how he got the cough, or colic. 1 have known a man seemingly in perfect health, who, by eating a penny worth of pastiy, as he passed along street, was seized with such an asthmatic fit, that he was obliged to be carried home, and had neai'- lv lost his life. This occurred whenever he inadvertently ateany thing baked with butler. Every thing that proves very injurious te health ought, as far as possi- i- ble, to be prohibited, bv laying a high duty up..n it. A duty on pastry I would be serving the public in mare respects than one. It would sa\ e many lives, and lessen some ta\ on mercuries. 390 OF FRUITS AND ROOT*. Cheese, as a diet, is likew ise injurious to health. It should uevei tie eaten but as a dessert. It occasions constipation, flies the blood, and excites a constant craving for drink. It is very improper for the seden- tary, and hardly to be digested even by the athletic. If men will live on dry bread, poor cheese, salt butter, broiled bacon, and such like parching food, they will find tlieir way to the ale-house,' the bane of the lower orders, and the source of half the beggary in thei nation. OF FRUITS AND ROOTS. FRUITS and roots form a large class of substitutes for bread. Tli? latter being produced under ground, are less liable to suffer from tlie inclemency of the seasons than grain. Men who wish to inflame the minds of the multitude may inveigh against the substitutes for bread; but reason and sound sense say, the more substitutes for bread the bet- ter. When one fails, recourse can be had to another. In warm climates the inhabitants have many substitutes for bread, and as their seasons are more uniform than ours, they can generally depend on the plant, or whatever it is, proving productive. The plantain-tree, commonly called the Indian-fig, which has from time immemorial been cultivated in South-America, hears fruit ofa sweetish taste, which will dissolve in tbe. month without chewing. It is eaten either raw, fried, i or roasted. When intended to supply the place of bread, it is gatliei ed before it is ripe, and eaten either boiled or roasted. The banana is nearly of the same nature, but its fruit is greatly superior, both in taste and flavour. The inhabitants of the South-Sea, or Ladrone islands, are supplied with bread from a tree, which has bein lately imported into our West- India islands, and will it is hoped, be found to answer the same pur- pose tliere. It has a slight degree of sweetness, but not much flavour. It resembles new bread, and requires to be roasted before itis eaten. Those who have tasted it say, that it is in no respect superior to tht potatoe. In some of the West India islands ihe inhabitants supply the place of grain by making bread from the root ot' a shrub called ihe ca.-sida, or cassava. Though, to my taste, this bread is very insipid, yet tlie natives are fond of it to such a degree that 1 have known some of tliem eat it, '!ur:ng their residence iu England, iu preference to the finest London bread. But the most general substitutes for bread in the West-Iudies are the yams. There are three different species of this plant, the roots »<f which are promiscuously used for bread. They are said to be very nu- tritions, of easy digestion, and when properly dressed, arc by some preferred to the best wheaten bread. The taste is somewhat like tlie potatoe, but more luscious. The negroes generally eat them boiled, and beaten into a mash. The white people have them ground into flour, and make bread and puddings or" them. They can be pre- served for several seasons, without losing any of tlieir primitive good- ness. Of all the substitutes for bread in Europe, the potatoe is the most ex- tensively useful. This plant is a native of Peru, aud has been in Kit- rope about two hundred years. Like most other important disrove* rie.s, it made bHt a slow progress, and is still far from being so ;■< n< rally cultivated as it deserves to be. It is indeed known in most paitiof Ku* rope, bat its culture is best understood in Ireland and the noi them 1>«" fl •f England. At Harwich, however, the preference is given to ihe.Dutcf OF FRUITS AND ROOTS. SSI potatoes,brought over by the packets between thatplaceand Helvoet Sluys. Tliere is a light sandy soil in Holland very favourable to the culture of that inestimable root. As this plant thrives in every soil, and seldom suffers from the incle- mency ofthe seasons, we must blame ourselves if we niffer a famine to exist. Indeed no such thing ever can be, where due attention is paid to the culture, of potatoes. A far greater quantity of farinaceous food can be raised on an acre of ground planted with potatoes, than sown with any kind of grain. It is not uncommon to have a return of forty for one. They are not so hearty a food as corn, but no man will ever perish for hunger who can have potatoes. Potatoes abound with an insipid juice, which induces some to think that they are not very nutritious. Facts, however, are against this opinion " Some of the. stoutest men we know, arc brouglit up on milk and potatoes. Dr. Pearson, who has bestowed some pains in an- alizmg this root, says, that potatoes and water alone, with common - salt, can nourish men completely. They differ in colour and consist- ence, but not materially with regard to their nutritive qualities. Some think the firm kind are the most nutritious; but the Irish, who must be good judges, give the preference to the mealy. The difference, however, depends much on the mode of cooking them. More than half the substance of potatoes consists of water, and ex- perience shows, that that mode of cooking, which most diminishes their moisture, is to be preferred. In London they are drenched in water r»nd washed before they are brought, to market, which accounts, in a great measure, for the bail quality ofthe London potatoes... They are dressed in a variety of ways, but simple boiling or roasting Mems to be all the rooking they require, to render them a pioper sub- stitute for bread. Some are fond of making bread of them. This, in my opinion, is marring both. Why manufacture any thing into bread, which requires only the aid of fire to make it such ? Nobody thinks of making dongli'of the bread t'rui ; but the potatoe might with as great propriety be called the bread root, as it is math- into bread by the lame pioce-s. Stewed mutton and po'atoes make not only a nourishing, but a very palatable dish. Theexoess of fat of the mutton, which when otherwise cooked sustains great loss is thus preserved, by being t.b orbed by the potatoes. Itis, however, to be ob.-ct ved, that when potatoes are used in broth or stews, they ought previously to be boiled, and ihe water thrown away, as it contains so :nc thing deleterious. Simple boiling or roaMing is sufficient to prepare potatoes to snpply the place of bread, but when they are intended to serve as a meal, they require something ofasoftening'nattire, as milk, butter, or both. What a.trea;ureis a milch cow and a potatoe garden, to a poor man with a large family, who lives in the country"! Yet, with a little attention from land- lords and farmers, almost eveiy man mi.lt be so accommodated. What a source of real wealth and population ! Men would multiply, and poverty, unless among the profligate, be unknown. Horses are sometimes fed with potatoes, and become very fond of them. V\ dn the addition of a small quantity of hay, they are found to be sufficiently nourishing. . .... . /• I would be" leave to recommend, both to landlords and farmers,* a cartful perusal of Earl Wmchelsea's excellent letter to Sir John Sm- elau, on the advantages of cottagers renting lands llns humane Nobleman takes up the matter in a truly patriotic light, and shews 382 OF FRUITS AND ROOTS. that farmers, instead of lessening the number of poor, do every thin; thi y can to multiply them , and I am sorry to say, that, so far as my observation goes, it agrees entirely with his lordship's. Some think that the potatoe, unless it is made into bread, will not keep. An accident taught me the contrary. Many years ago a friend of mine sent me a potatoe, after it had been roasted in an oven, on ac« count of its singular figure. I laid it on a shelf among some otiier tiring* of the like kind, and was surprised on removing tlieni many years after, to find the potatoe quite fresh though as dry as a bone. On grating it down it was perfectly sweet; and as fit for making soup as the day it was roasted. I apprehend that nothing made into bread would have kept so long. Posterity will hardly believe that a scarcity of bread could be felt in Britain, at a time when it was known that a sufficient quantity of farinaceous food could be raised in one county for the inhabitants of the whole island. Let proper encouragement be given to the culture of po- tatoes, and set famine at defiance. Many otiier domestic roots, sprouts, &c. are very wholesome and may occasionally supply the place of bread. Of these Mi. Bryant of Norwich, reckons above, forty ; but we shall only take notice, by way- of specimen, ofthe most useful and productive. Itis worthy ofre-< mark, that no nation can be very populous, which does not draw a< great part of its food from under ground. ' The Jerusalem artichoke is a native of Brazil; hut, having been long ' cultivated in this country, it is too well known to need any desci ip'ioR. From its taste, which is like that of artichoke bottoms, it would seem to be nutritious,and is far from being unpleasant to the palate. Some reckon it windy, but this may be corrected in the cooking, by warm spices, and as the plant is very productive, we would iccoiiiniond it to be used in the same manner as potatoes and the other farinaceous roots. Of the esculent roots in this country, the parsnip is reckoned the most nourishing. It is likew ise of easy digestion, and is agreeable to most palates. Some indeed dislike it on account of its sweetness ;' but that is a proof of its nutritive quality, sugar being the most nourishing thing in nature. We are told that, in the north ot* Ireland, the poor people make/beerfrom this root. There is not any plant that affords a more striking proof ofthe bene- fits of culture than the turnip. In its wild state it i.-> good for little or nothing; but, when properly cultivated, it not only affords wholesome nourishment for man, but furnishes tbe principal winter food lor cattle. Tliere is a species of this plant which grows in North Britain, called the yellow turnip, which is sweet, and of a superior quality to those pro- duced in the south, particularly about London, which arc bitter and stringy. The yellow turnip is the most nourishing, and also the most hardy in sustaining the winter. It is eaten with milk to cure die con- sumption and scurvy. Margraafsays, In; could extract no sugar from the turnip, which aiiords ground to conclude, that it is not so nutritive as certain other roots. Not only the root of the turnip, but tlie tops, when young, make very pleasant greens. The sprouts, if gathered when very tender, make an excellent salad. The carrot, like the turnip, is good for little in its natural state, 1* ing small, tough and stringy. Manured, it grows large/ succulent, and of a pleasant flavour. It ought, however, to be cMten young, odierwipj it lies on the stomach, and is bard of digestiotyjdflfc wn ingredient ■ OF FRUITS AND ROOTS. 33? se?>ral soups, and being solid, may in some measure supply the place of bread. Sal«afy,skirrets,and the several kinds of beets, are all pleasant and nourishing. They are likewise of easy digestion, and may be dressed in a variety of ways. Margraaf has by experiments discovered, that uoth skuiets and beets contain a considerable quantity of sugar. Though the exnactinga saccharine salt from these plants may be no object while we possess the West-India islands, yet it serves to shew .hat they possess a quantity of nutritious matter, sufficient togive tlieni a rank anion" the articles calculated o supply the place of bread. The onion, we are told, was a great favourite in Egypt four thousand Tcaisago,andI)r. Hasselqtiest says, it is not to be wondered at, for whoever has tasted the onions of Egypt must allow, that none can be better in any part ofthe globe. There, he says, they are sweet, though in many countries they are strong and nauseous. Tliere they are sott, whereas in northern countries they are hard ; and their coat so com- pact, that they are difficult to digest. This very quality may however recommend them in couiitiie- where food is scarce. Ihe Doctor ob- se.ves that the Tnrkrtat them roasted with their meat as we do bread, and are so loud of them that they wish to be indulged with this dish in paradise. «u„nu„ From the Doc-tor's account one would be induced to believe, that the onion used in Egypt was ofa different species from ours; but I am rather inclined to think it may depend on the mode of culture, as well as on the warmth of climate and the difference of soil, as we find in the ■■ southern parts of Europe they are milder than in the more northerly. Ho Spain they are very mild, and a root weighing two pounds will grow mom a single seed. . IF Onions are dressed in a variety of ways, but, in regard to whole- someness, there is no method better than simple boiling. By this me- thod of cooking, they are rendered mild, of easy digestion, and go oft witliout leaving any disagreeable heat in the stomach or bowels. Ma- ny shun them ou account ofthe strong disagreeable smell they commu- ■icate to the breath. Mr Bryant says, this may be remedied, by eating a lew raw parsley leaves immediately after, which will eflectuaby over- come the scent of the onions, and likewise cause them to sit more easy on the stomach. . . , M A. . . Tli-leek is generally reckoned among pot-herbs ; but as the root is the part chiefly used, the consideration ofit comes under the present head of discussion. Indeed, it is as properly a root as the onion, which grows chiefly above ground. The leek, as well as the onion, is said to be a constant dish at the tables of the Egyptians, who chop them small, andcat them with their meat. ,„-,.. • .. • The leek is used as a pot-herb in most parts of Britain, especially in Wales, where the natives are said to be fond of it. In Scotland a full pown fowl and a small piece of salt beef, stewed with a large quantity Mifleeks.is a very favourite dish. In my opinion the leek isuotsogen- erallv „ ed anv where as,t deserves to be There is no mpedien tgoes into soup tl.alV more wholesome, or that gives it a better flavour, than leeks They are in many respects medicinal, and to my taste, as an in- p-ftlimt in soups, they are greatly supeuor to the onion, or any other ""i'tTs'a tacf vXrtiiy of observation, that the boiling of vegetable sub- Mantes flioroughlv, cMr.cates., consideiablc quantiy ot a.r, and make. l.:s.ui less liable to produce flatulency. Sil OF BROTHS AND SOUPS. I could mention a great many more esculent plants which might oc- casionally supply the place of bread, but the above spec mien is sufficient to shew how liberal nature is in supplying man with food, provided he will take the trouble of cultivating and cooking it. Mr. Brvaiit, in his history of esculent plants, enumerates above four hundred and fifty, each of which affords a wholesome nourishment ; aud may occasional- ly be used in place of bread. OF BROTHS AND SOUPS. THESE may likewise be considered as substitutes for bread. If properly made they will serve both for bread and drink. Though broth is a dish of the greatest antiquity, and maybe considered as extremely delicious, yet it is not a favourite in this country. Here the people are fond of what they call solids ; yet those very solids they make in'o broth by swallowing as much drink after them as they can get. The only difference is, the foreigner makes his broth in a pot, and the Englishman makes his in the stomach. A very sensibly anonymous writer observes, that in England a pound ofmeat makes simply a pound of food ; whereas in any other country in Europe, that quantity of animal food, when stewed down with vege- tables and Scotch barley, will produce an ample meal for half a dozen people. Hence he justly infers that among ihe variety of schemes wliich may have been devised by the humane for relieving the distresses ofthe poor, a better and more extensive charity cannot be devised than that of instructing them in a new mode of cookery. The same author adds, that the result of his experiments on this sub- ject had exceeded his most sanguine expectations, and that each day gave him fresh proofs ofthe excellency of his plan for teaching the poor and needy to find themselves in a wholesome and palatable diet at the cheapest rate, in which little or no bread was required. He concludes by asserting that there is scarce a place in this kingdom where twenty- persons may not have a wholesome, hearty, and palatable meal for three shillings. The writer who has paid most attention to the improvement of cook- ery for the benefit of the poor, is Count Rumford. In hi- economical and philosophical essays, he has given such a variety of forms for making wholesome, cheap and nourishing soups, stews, and other dishes for common use that little more seems necessary to be said on the subject. I shall only observe that the mode of living on broth , soups, hasty pud- ding, and such like, so warmly and justly recommended by the Count, has been practised in the northern parts of this kingdom from time im- memorial. There the food ofthe common people is hasty-pudding with milk for breakfast aud supper, and broth, with vegetables and meat, for dinner. The poorer t-ort often make broth without meat ; but they all use vegetables in great abundance, and sometimes tin y supply the place ofmeat with butter. As the hasty pudding and milk make a complete meal, no bread is necessary either at supper or breakfast; nor i- much required at dinner, as the broth is made thick with barley, cabbage, and a variety of other vegetables or pot In rbs. Cabbage i> a favounte ingredient in the Scotchman's broth. It is seldom made without this ar- ticle, wliich is not eaten so early as in England. It is there suffered tu grow to maturity, and when that is the ease, there is no plant more pro- ductive. This the Germans know well, and make it into sour crout, one of the best antidotes against the scurvy with which we are ac- quainted. This kind of diet not only saves bread but drink. The labourer who ,4 OF BROTHS AND SOUPS. *-■> lives on hasty-pudding and soups, seldom has occasion for drink ; while he who is burnt up with dry bread and cheese, or salt meat broiled, has a continual thirst, and spends the greater part of his earn- ings in liquor. This, by acting as a powerful stimulus, may make him do more work for sometime, but it generally cuts him off in the middle of his days. The English labourer, who works hard and drinks hard, seldom lives long, and is an old mau when he should be in his prime. The roasting ofmeat is a wasteful mode of cookery, which ought to be avoided by the poorer sort ofpeople,as much of the substance, and the most nntiitive parts are lost by scorching, and fly off by evapo- ration. . n ... e . 1 know it will be said, that I recommend slops in place of solid food. They are such slops, however, as the greatest heroes of antiquity lived upon ; and though I have visited most parts ofthe island, I know of no better men than those who live in the manner described above, nor are the people any where moreliealthy, or longer lived. Broth is not only a dish of great antiquity, but one that can be made in a great variety of ways. It receives into its composition animal and vegetable substances of every kind that are used in diet, and it may be seasoned so as to suit every palate. Indeed, people early accustomed to eat broths properly made, are generally fond of them for their whole It would be difficult to assign a reason why the inhabitants of South Britain sliould dislike a dish so much relished by other nations. Cus- torn, no doubt, settles all these things ; but how customs arise is not so clear a matter. If an alteration in diet is to be introduced with effect, it must begin with children. Whatever men are accustomed to eat •when young, they generally prefer for the rest of their lives. Mere 'the children in South Britain taught to eat hasty-pudding, with mnk, for breakfast and supper; and broth with vegetables and meat boiled in it for dinner, they would relish these dishes as long as they lived, 'would find little occasion for bread, and still less for drink ; and would thrive better than on their present food. What parents love themselves, they generally give to their children, without any regard to its beingproper for them or not. i have seen a Hither, who was fond of strong beer, make his son, an intuit, guzzle it at every meal ; aud the mother who delights in tea, docs not fail to give it to her daughter whenever she takes it to herself. By this con- duct, the son becomes a tippler, aud the daughter sips tea in the place of solid food, until she is eaten up with vapours and other nervous d:s- Cou'ut Rumford says, brown soup is the common breakfast of the Ba- varian peasants, to which they occasionally add bread. This he avers is infinitely preferable in all respects to that pernicious wash, tea, with which the lower classes of the inhabitants of this island drench their stomachs, and ruin their constitutious. He adds, that a Miiyle infu- sion of this drug, drank boiling hot, as the poor generally drink it, is certainly poison, which, though it be sometimes slow in its operation, never fails to produce fatal effects, even in the strongest constitution, where the free use ofit is continued for a considerable length of time. The German on his polenta, the Americau on his musA, and the North Briton on his hasty-pudding, can make a In-ai.y breakfast for a tenth part of what a tea-break fast would cast, wl.n.- it is infinitely more wholesome It has likewise the advantage that no bread is necessary. I have been often told, when recommending soups to the :>uor, that Kk 3 Sd CONCLUSION. tltfy had no time to make them, and that they could not afford tail on account of its price, as it is dear in great towns. They can how- ever, find fuel twice a-day to boil a tea-kettle, and time to make the tea, which is a more tedious operation, by far, than makuig a mess of hasty pudding. For a great part of the year even the poorest person must have a little fire ; and it would require no more to make a com- fortable mess of soup, which is always best when made with a slow fire. The mode of living that I would recommend to the lower orders of the people, with a view to save expense and improve their health, is to substitute occasionally other farinaceous substances in the place «f bread, as potatoe, &c. to give up in a great measure the use of roasted, baked, and broiled meats, and to supply their place with broths, soups, stews, and such like, made with a little meat and plenty of vegetables; to give to children, and to grown people who will eat it, for breakfast, milk-porridge, or hasty-pudding with milk, small beer, or molasses. This will be found a more wholesome breakfast, than tea, while it is much cheaper and requires no bread.* CONCLUSION^ AFTER a long attention to the cure of diseases, it is mortifying to reflect how much this arduous province is infested by a race of ignorant and shameless empirics, who are daily tampering with the public cre- dulity, to the destruction of numbers of lives. It may safely be af- firmed, that a considerable part of the annual deaths are occasioned by the profligate temerity of these unprincipled impostors. There is hardly a news-paper that does not teem with the audacious falsehoods, and pompous pretensions, of this imposing class of mercenary, and yet (I use not too harsh an expression) tolerated murderers. What man who is conversant w i'h physic can peruse without indignation the pub- lic advertisements of these quacks in which every one arrogates to him- self the possession of superlative knowledge, and ascribes to his respec- tive nostrum such contradictory and inconsistent qualities as were never yet united in any one medicine in the world. To the disgrace, however, of the public credulity, not a few of these impostors attain to a degree of opulence that is seldom acquired evti in the scientific and legitimate prosecution of medical practice. The artifices which they employ to delude the multitude are well known to many. Having picked up the name of some extremely ac- tive medicine, the bold and indiscriminate use of which mast therefore be proportionally dangerous, they immediately resolve on converting * The celebrated Dr. Huffland, in his Art of prolonging Life, says, a moderate use of soups is certainly not hurtful; and it is singular that people should imagine-it tends too much to relax the stomach. Does not all our drink, even though cold, become in • few minutes a kind of warm soup in the itomach ; and does not the stomach Main the same temperature during the whole day ? Be careful only not to use it hot, in too great quantity at one time, or too watery. It is attended even with great advantage.. It supplies the place of drink, particularly to men of letters, women, and all those who do drink very little except at table, and who, whan they give o, er soup, n ceive into their blood too little moisture. And it is here to be remarked, that fluid* used in the form of soups unite much better and sooner with our juices than when drunk cold and raw. On this account soup is a great preventive of dryness and rigidity in the body; and therefore the best nourishment for old people, and those who are of an arid tempera- ment. It even supplies the place of medicine. After catching cold, in nervous hi an- aches, colics, and different kinds of cramp in the stomach, warm toup is of excellent service It may serveas a proof of the utility, or at least harmless ness of soap, when I remark dial our forefathers, who certainly had more strength than we have, u.ed soup; and that it is used by rustics, who are still stronger than tho*. in refined life; aad that all the oW people with whom I ever was acquainted were great friends to it. CONCLUSION. «47 j it into a nostrum, and endeavour to disseminate its unrivalled praises either by advertisements or hand-bills. But being themselves totally j, illiterate, they have, for this purpose, recourse to some other pei>o«; whom they engage for a stipulated reward to fabricate the pernicious . illusion. A hyperbolical panegyric on the wonderful remedy is ac- cordingly vamped up, and preparations are made for commencing a lucrative trade with the public. Sliould the channel of communication be the public papers, it is a settled point, that it' daily or frequent i advertisements can be supported for the space if some months, the. ;■ fame of the medicine, whatever be its real character, is established. Tbe better to promote this purpose, innumerable authorities in favour j ofthe nostrum are asserted in general terms; venality is a^ain exerted ■ to furnish .specific testimonials in it- support; and if, among the Lumber of unfortunate purchasers, or patients, tliere exists any per-on nho has not only taken it with impunity, but even with some advr u- tagc, (and what extremely powerful medicine may not sometimes by chance have good effects'?-) the fortuitous incident is immediately bla- zoned with all the ostentation of interested zeal and affected popular- ity ; and a reference to unco.nipted testimony resounded through J' every channel of information. Ey a strange association, truth now is confidently adduced iu support of falsehood ; anil the recovery of one or two persons is rendered the unhappy means ff draining the purse, undermining the health, and destroying the lives of thousands. Such, in fact, is the general progress of empiricism. Were the task not invidious, and the objects too despicable for any other than juridi- cal cognizance, which they merit in a superlative degree, the represen- tation here given might be supported by unquestionable authority. It ¥" Is hoped, however, that enough has been said to influence the minds of the judicious with respect to this iniquitous practice, wliich becomes every day more alarming, and threatens tbe more credulous part of the l community with the most fatal effects. This country, through the blessing of Providence, has been ex- empted from the horrors of famine, and for years -.-f the sword; but th« infatuation ofa numerous body of the people has subjected it to the ravages of another public calamity, which, though generally more slow in its operation than any ofthe former, is equally destructive in the end. Humanity shudders at the horrible depredations committed on the human constitution by this empirical tribe, who subsist by public delusion, and riot, where they can, in the irreparable ruin of those whom they entice into tlieir snares. What consumptive visages, what enfeebled frames, what mutilated bodies, and what palsied limbs, are the miserable monuments of that ignorance and criminalstnrerity by which they are actuated ! ff"P*> In certain diseases, itis doubtless an object of impoi tance^thehn- fortnnate patients, that their cure should be conducted with secrecy, and likewise to many, at the smallest possible expense ; but they do not consider that, while they arc economical in this article, they are fatally prodigal of health. They grasp with eagerness the pill- box or the phial which they are assured contains the elixir of speedy and effectual convalescence ; but, alas! the flattering hope proves of shaft duration. They may feel perhaps, for a little, a suppressMn of the symptoms of their disease; but the destructive embers|He "smo- thered, not extinguished; and, while preying upon the vitals, are acquiring a maliguity which will again, break forth with redoubled vio- lence. S38 , CONCLUSION. It is not however, in one disease only, nor in the lower class ofthe people, that I his infatuated credulity operates ; wc find it prevail even amongst those from whose superior situations in life more discernment might be expected ; but who have nevertheless becomevoluntarv dupes t > the meanest artifices of empiricism. Witness the successful impos- ture practised with regard to the inspection of urine ; the visionary notion of charms, &<\ lint it is time that such chimerical doctrines should he consigned to the regions of barbaiism, and flourish no longer in a s»il where almost every other physical rrrejiidice has been rooted up and explored by the progress of science. To effect this salutary purpose, nothing can have t more powerful tendency than the view winch has been given, in the preceding p^sres. of the causes and cure of diseases. By removing tbe mysterious veil which for a !oii£ time concealed this useful branch of knowledge from the eyes of the public, it ought, on one hand, to pre- el jdnfortver all resources to empirical impostors, and, on the other, to show in v hat cases it will be proper to call in the assistance ofa physi- cian. W ithiu thebouuds prescribed by this limitation any person of an ordinary capacity may act in conformity to the rules wliich have been delivered. By this means a prudent economy will be consulted, un- happy patients will no longer be shipwrecked on the dangerous rocks of empiricism, with all their deceitful allurements, but will be conducted tbrcngh the saft-st aud most direct road to the recovery of health, when that desirable ol ject is practicable. " All I in what perils is vain life engag'd I What slight neglects, what trivial faults destroy The hardiest frame I Of indolence, of toil, Wc die; of want, of suptifluity : The all-surrounding heaven, the vital air. Is big with death. And though tlie putrid south Be shut; though no convulsive agony Shake, from the deep foundations of the world, Th' imprison'd plagues; a secret venom oft Coimpts the air, the water, and the land." APPENDIX: CONTAINING A List of simples and of such Medicinal prorations as ought to be kept in readiness for private Practice. The method of preparing and compounding such foidicines as are recom- mended in the former Part of the Book, with th* Addition of several others of a similar Nature. Remarks on the Doses, Uses, and Manner of applying uie different Preparations. Medicamentorum varietas ignorentix filia est.—Bacon. INTRODUCTION. IGNORANCE and superstition have attributed extraordinary medical virtues to al- most every production of nature. That such virtues were often imaginary, time and experience have sufficiently shewn. Physicians, however, from a veneration for anti- quity, still retain in their lists of medicine many things which owe their reputation en- tirely to the superstition and credulity of our ancestors. The instruments of medicine will always be multiplied, in proportion to men's igno- rance of the nature and cure of diseases ; whan these are sufficiently inderstood, the mitliod of cure will be simple and obvious. Ignorance ofthe real nature and permanent properties of those substances employed! intnecureof diseines, is another reason why thef Mve been so greatly multiplied. 1'lijYicians thought they could effect by a number of ingredients, what could not be done by any one of them. Hence arose those amazing farragos which have so long di.graced the medical art, and which were esteemed powerful in proportion to the number of simples that entered their composition. The great variety of forms into which almost every article of medicine has been ins nufactured, affords another proof of the imperfection of the medical art. A drug which b perhaps most efficacious in the simplest form in which it can be administered, haa been nevertheless served up in so many .lifiereni shapes, that one would be induced t« think the whole art of physic lay in exhibiting medicine under as many different modes is possible. Different forms of medicine, no doubt, have their use; but they ought never to be ' Wantonly increased. They are bv no means so ncces-ary as is gem-rally irnagiued. A few grains of powdered rhubarb,*.jalap, or ipecacumha, will actually perform all that can be done by the different preparations of these roots, r.nd u.av also be exhibited in as safe and agreeable a manner. The same observation holds with reg:ird to the Peruvian bark, and many other simples, of which the preparations art- very nume- rous. Multiplying the ingredients of a medicine, not only renders it more expensive, but also less certain, both in its dose and operation. Nor is this all. The compound, when kept, is apt to spoil, or acquire qualities of a different nature. When a medicine is ren- der.d more safe, efficacious, or agreeable, by the addition of another, they oua.bt, no doubt, to be joined ; in all other cases, they are better kept asunder. 1 he combination of medicines embarrasses the physician, and retards the prog.-ess of medical knowledge. It is impossible to ascertain the"precise effect of any one vueuicine.as lonyns it is com- bined with others.citber of a similar or dissimilar nature. .,.-.,., In the exhibition of medicine, regard should not only be had to simplicity, but like- wiie to elegance. Patients seldom reap much benefit from things that are highly disa- greeable to their senses. To Uste or smell like a drug, is become a proverb: and to say truth, there is too much ground for it. Indeed no art can take awny thj disagreeable taste and flavour of somedrugs, without entirely destrox ing tnen-eft^.tey: it_is possible, however, to render many medicines less disgustful, and others even agreeable; anob- ject highly deserv ing the attention of all who administer mtii.ci...-. The design ofthe following pages is, to exhibit such a list ol drugs and ir.td.euies a. may be necessary for private practice. 'I hey are conjide rably more numerous in- dc-d than those recommended in the former part ol the book, but are still greatly within the number contained in the most reformed dispensatories. 1 he same medicine is sel- dom exhibited underdiffere.it forms; and where different medicine* answer nearly the same intention, there it con.moaly no inure than one of them it tamed. . MuL tinlvine- forms of medicine for the same intention lends rather to bewilder than assist the young practitioner,and the experienced physician can never be at a los- to vary ,i; ^swptioM»sv*W»« requires. o 3?d APPENDS- The chemical and other difficult papar*-"0"* are for the m0't P*n ?",iueJ,- A" ofthcm that are used by any private p-ctltioner are not worth preparuig. He will buy them much cheaper rli.u. be can -&**iUtnu Grcat wrv however is iieccis.iy to obtsin them genuine. They ar.-open adulterated, and ought never to bo purchased unless from persousof known verity- s"cl» of tin mas are in common use, are mierted in the list of drugs ami medic*1"- Their proper doses, and manner ol application, are mentioned in the practir*« P"1 of the nool<. wherever they are prescribed. Such articles ot'raediciii* •* nrt"t0 he found in the housa or garden of almost ever? peasant, a« barley egrs onion., Jkc. are likewise for the most part omitted. Itis need- 1 -si to swell a li-t ofY«edicines with such things as can be obtained whenever they are wanted, and which »!)«■» br being kept. ... , .. . «. The preparati'115 made and sold by distillers and confectioners are also pcnn-ally left out. TheserKopW by operating upon a larger plan, generally make things better, while it is i« **l',r power to afford tlieni much cheaper than they can be prepared by any priv»«-e hand. *p^e.iU»ntityorderedof every medicine is as small as well could be prepared, both to prev. nt unnecessary expense, and that the medicine might not spoil by keening. /VJiu**t every medicine suffers by being kept, and should be used as soon after it has Dean , v-pare-d as possible. Even simple drugs are apt to spoil, and should therefore be laid in •n small quantities ; they either rot, are consumed by insects, or evaporate so as to lose ilieir peculiar taste or flavour, and often bccoiMonite insignificant. In the preparations of medicines, I have generally followed the most approved diipm- •atories; hut have taken the liberty to differ from them whenever my own observations, or those of other practical writers, on whose judgrnemt 1 could depend, suggested an improvement. In several compositions, the ingredient on which the efficacy of the medicine chiefly depends is increased, while the auxiliaries, which are generally ordered in such trifling quantities as to be of no importance, are left out, or only such of them retained ns are necessary to give tbe medicine a proper consistence or the like. The colouring ingSViuents at»likewise for the most part omitted. They increase the I i ice and bulk ofthe inedicineY'yMhout adding any thing to its value. It would be .veil if they w«j never used at all.TWedicines are often adulterated for the sake ofa valour. Acrid and even poisonous substances are, for this purpose, sometimes introdu- i r.l into those medicines w Inch ought to be most bland and emollient. Verdigrease, for r xninple, is often mixed with ointment of elder to give it a fine green colour, wliich en- u-elv frustrates tho intention of that mild ointment. Those who wish to obtain genuine medicines sliould pay no regard to their colour. Some regard is likewise paitl to expense. Such-ingredients as greatly increase the price of any composition, without adding considerably to its virtue.are generally either omitted, or soiiiewhat-k si expensive substituted in their place. Medicines are by no means puwi rful in proportion to their price. The cheapest are often the best, be- sides, ihey are the leirst :i;il to be adulterated, and are always most ready to be obtained. With regard to the method of com pounding medicines, 1 hnve generally followed that whieli seemed to be the most simple and natural, mentioning the different steps of the process in the same m-J ,r in which they ought to be taken, without paying an implicit n-yard to the method of other dispensatories. Foi many of tin- remarks concerning the preparation. &c. of medicines, 1 hare been thliged to the author of the New Dispensatory. The other observations are either such ::s hare occurred to myself in practice, or have been suggested in the course t»f reacl- .Bg. by authors whose names I am n»t able distinctly to recollect. I have (olio weil the alphabetical order, both with regard to the simples and prepara- tions. A more scientific method would have been agreeaWle to some persons, but less useful to the generality of readets. The different classes of medicine have no great .Upendence upon one another, and, where they hare, it is hard to say which should stand first oelast; nodoubt the simple preparations ought to precede the more com- pound. But all the advantages arising from this method of arrangement, do not appear to be equal to that single one, of being able, on tlie first opening of the book, to find out tiny article, which, by the alphabetical order, is rendered quite easy. The dose of every mediciiwis mentioned whenever it appeared neces-.tin-. When this is omitted it is to be understood that tlie medicine may be used at discretion. The ■ i ita mentioned is always for an adult,unless when the contrary is ex p. ss> il. It is not an easy matter to proportion the doses of medicine exactly to tho different ages, con- stitutions. &c. of patients; but, happily fur mankind, mathematical exactness is by no means necessary. Several attempts have been made to ascertain the proportional doses for the different nges and constitutions of patients ; but after all that can be said on this subject, a fp-eat cu-al must be left to tlie judgment ami skill ofthe person who administers the nntdicine. Thefbl'oiviot- general proportions may be observed ; but they are by no means intend- ed for exact rules. A patient between twenty and fourteen may take two thirds of the dose ordered for an adult; from fourteen to idnr. one half; fro.u nine to six, one sixth ; from two to one, a tenth ; and below one, a twelfth. Dispe-nsatcries art; nsually wriittn in the Latin language. Even authors who write APPENDIX. 3-Jl in English, generally give their prescriptions in Latin ; and some of them shew 10 great an attachment to that language-, as fust to write their recipes in it; and afterwards translate them; while others, to compromise the matter, writcthe one half in Latin and the other in English. What peculiar charm a medical prescription, when written in Latin, may have, I shall not pretend to say; but have ventured to make use ofthe plainest English I oould, and hope my prescriptions will succeed no worse for it. N. B The Apothecary's weights, and the English wine nv-asiires, are used through- out the whole book, the different denominations of which ivill appear from thi follow- ing Table:— A pound contains twelve ounces. An ounce - - eight drachms. A drachm - - three scruples. A scruple - - twenty grains. A gallon contains eight pints A pint - - sixteen ounces. An ounce - - eight drachms.
