A table spoonful is the measure of half an ounce, A tea spoonful is one fourth of a table spoonful. Sixty drops make one tea-spoonful. The following Table of doses for different Ages, is given by Dr. Thomson. [The Common Dose being taken at one Drachm.] Parts of the Proportions Common Dose. of a Drachm. BALSAMS. THE subject of this section is not thd natural balsams, but certain composiii6nt,, which, from their being supposed to possess balsamic qualities, generally go I.y that name. This class of medicines was formerly very numerous, and held in great esteem; modern practice, however, has justly reduced it to a very narrow compass. Anodyne Balsam.—Take of white Spanish soap, one ounce; opium, unprepared, two drachms ; rectified spirit of wine, tune ounces. Digest them together in a gentle heat tor three days, then strain off the liquor, and add to it three drachms of camphor. This balsam, as its title cxpri-Mcs, is intended to ease pain. It is of strvice in violent strains, aud rheumatic complaints, when not attended with inflammation. It must be rubbed with a warm hand on the .part affected ; ora linen rag moistened with it may be applied to the part, and renewed every third or fourth hour, till the pain abates. If the opium is left out, this will be the Saponaceous Balsam. Located?* Balsam.—Take of olive oil, one pint; Strasburg turpentine and yellow wax, of each half a pound; red sauitders, six drachms. Melt the wax with some part of the oil over a gentle fire; then adding the remaining part of the oil and the turpentine; afterwards mix in the sawders previously reduced to a powder, and keep thern stirring together till the balsam is cold. This balsam is recommended in erosions of the intestines, the dysentery, hemorrha- ge's, internal bruises, and in some complaints ofthe breast. Outwardly it is used for healing and cleansing wounds aud ulcers. The dose, when taken internally, is from two scruples to two drachms. Tlie vulnerary Balsam*--Take of benzoin, powdered, three ounces; balsam of Peiu, two ounces; hepatic aloes, in powder, half an ounce ; rectified spirit of wine, two piiit-. Digest them in a gentle heat for three days, and then strain tlie Balsam. This Balsam, or rather tincture, is applied externally to heal recent wounds and bruises. It is likewise employed internally to remove coughs, asthmas, and other com- plaints of the breast. It is said to ease tbe colic, cleanse the kidnies, and to heal inter- nal ulcers, Sic. Thedoseis from twenty to sixty drops. This, though a medicine of some value, does not deserve the extravagant encomium! vbicUnave U«a bestowed ou it. It has oven celebrated under the diil'eiuit names «1 APPENDIX. .:9i The Commander's Balsam, Persian Balsam, Balsam of Eerne, Wade's Balsam, Friar's Balsam, Jesuit's Drops,Turlington's Drops, tire. tOLUSES. AS boluses are intended fur immediate use, volatile salts and other ingrcdimts im- proper for being kept, are admitted into tlieir composition. They are generally com- pojed of powders, with a proper quantity of syrup, conserve, or mucilage. The lighter powders are- commonly made up with syrup, and the more ponderous, as mercury, fee. with conserve; but those ofthe lighter kind would be more conveniently made- up with mucilage, as it increases tbeirbulk less than the other additions, and likewise occasions the medicine to puss down more easily- Astringent Bolus.—Take of alum, in powder, fifteen grains; gum kino, five grains; syrup, a sufficient quantity to make a bolus. In an excessive flow of the menses, and other violent discharges of blood^roseeding from relaxation, this bolus may be given every four or five hours, till the discharge abates. Diaphoretic Bolus.—Take of gum guaiacum, in powder, ten grains; flowers of sulphur and cream of tartar, ofeach one scruple; simple syrup, n sufficient quantity. • In rheumatic complaints, and disorders of the skin, this bolus may be taken twice a> day. It will also be of service in the inflammatory quinsey. Mirvrial Bolus.— Take of calomel, six grains; conserve of roses,half a drachm. M.:.' a bolus. Where mercury is necessary, this bolus may be taken twice or thrice a-wcek. It may he taken over night; and if it does not operate, a few grains of jalap will be proper next day to carry it off. Bolus of Rhubarb and Mercury.—Take ofthe best rhubarb, in powder, frara a scruple ro halfa drachm ; of calomel, from four to six grains; simple syrup, a sufficient quanti- ty to make a bolus. This is a proper purge in hypochondriac constitutions ; but its principal intention is to expel worms. Where a stronger purge is necessary, jalap may be used instead of the rhubarb. Pectoral Bolus.—Take of spermaceti, a scruple; srum ammoniac, ten grains ; salt of hartshorn, six grains; simple tyrup, &• much as will make them into a bolus. This bolus is given in colds aud coughs of long standing, asthmas, and beginning consumptions of the lungs. It is generally proper to bleed the patient before he begins louse it. Purging Bolus.—Take of jalap, in powder, a scruple; cream of tartar, two scruples. Let them be rubbed together, and formed into a bolus, with simple syrup. Where a mild ptu-ge is wanted, this will answer the purpose very well. Ifa stronger dose is necessary, the jalap may be increased to halt'adrachm or upwards. CATAPLASMS AND SJNAPISMS. CATAPLASMS possess few or no virtues superior to a poultice, which may be so ^ W>de, as, in most cases, to supply their place. They are chiefly intended either to act ' as discuticnts, or to promote suppuration; and as they may be of service iu some cases, we shall give a specimen of each kind. Discuttent Cataplasm.—Take of barley-meal, six ounces; fresh beml ock leaves, bruised, two ounces; vinegar, a sufficient quantity. Boil the meal at-.d hemlock in the vinegar for a little, and then add two drachms ofthe sugar of lead. Ripening Cataplasm.—Take of white lily root, four ounces; fat figs and raw onions, bruised, of each one ounce ; yellow basilicum ointment, two ounces, gum galbanum, half an ounce ; linseed meal, as much as necessary. Boil the roots along with the figs in a sufficient quantity of water; then bruise and add to them the other mgredieuts, so as to fbrm the whole into a soft cataplasm. The galbanum must be- previously dissolved with the yolk of an egg. Where it is necessary to promote suppuration, this cataplasm may be used by those who abuse to be at the trouble and expense of making it. For my part, I have never found any application more proper Car this purpose than a poultice ol bread and milk, -with :i sufficient quantity of either boiled or raw onion in it, and softened with oil or flesh butter. Sinapisms.—Sinapisms are employed to recal theblood and spirits to a w«k part, ns iu the palsy and atrophy. They are also of service in deep seated pains, as the sciatica, fcc. When the gout seizes the head or the stomach, they are applied to the feet to briiief the di» jrder to these parts. They are likewise applied to the patient's soles in the low <tate of fevers. They should not be suffered to fie on, however, till they have raised blisters, but till tbe parts become red,and will continue so when pressed with the tingir. The sinapism is only a poultice made with vinegar instead of milk, and rendered warm aud stimulating by the addition of mustard, horse-radish, or garlio. 'Ihe common sinapism is made by taking crumb of bread ana mustard-seed in powder, of each equal quantities ; strong vinegar, as much as is sufficient, and mixing then! so as to make a poultice. When sinapisms, ofa more stimulating nature are wanted, a liule bruised garlic may be added to the above. 394 APPENDIX. THIS class of med.eines is of more importance than is generally imagined. Clys- ters serve not only to evacuate the contents of the belly, but also to convey very tie- live mi die'ui'-s into the system. Opium, for example, ma, be administered in this way wh«i it will not sit upon the stomach, find also in larger doses than at anv tiaii it can be taken bj the mouth. The Perns ian bark may likewi..- be, with good i-dccl, ad> ministered in form of clyster to persons w ho cannot take it by the mouth. A simple clyster can seldom do hurt, and there are many ea.cs where it may do mnrti good. A clyster even of warm water, by serving as a fomentation 10 the parts, may be of considerable service in inflammations ofthe bladder,and the lower intestines, fce. Some substances, as the smoke of tobacco, may be thrown into the bowels in this way, which cannot by any other means whatever. '1 hi- may cr.silv be eflect.d by means of a pair of hand-bellows with an apparatus fitted to them for'lhat purpose. Nor is the use Of clysters contined to medicines. Aliin. nt may also be couvejidisj this way. Persons unable to swallow, have been, for a considerable time, supported by clysters. ^moll'cnt Clyster.—Take of linseed tea and new milk, each six onnces. Mix them. If fifty or sixty drops of laudanum be added to this, it will supply the place of the Anodyne Clyster. Laxative Clyster.—Take of milk anil warVreach six ounces ; sweet-oil or fresh but- ter, and brown sngnr,of each two ounces. Mix thi in. If an ounce of Glauber's salt, or two table »po mjful of common salt, be added to this, it will be the Purging Clyster. Carminative Clyster.—-Take of camomile flowers, an ounee ; anise-seeds, half an •unce. Boil in a pint and a half of water to ene pint. In hysteric and hypochondriac complaints thi* may be administered instead of the Tictid Cly.*ter,the smell of which is so disagreeable to most patients, Oily Clyster.—-To four ounces of the infusion of camomile flowers, add an equal quantity of Florence oil. This clyster is bencticial.in bringing off tbe small worms lodged in the lower parts s»f the alimentary canal. When given to children the quantity must be proporuona- bly lessened. Starch Clyster*—Take jelly of starch, four ounces; linseed oil, half an ounce. Li- quify the jelly over a gentle fire, and then mix in the oil. In the dysentery orbluody flux, this clyster may be administered after every loose stool, to beal the ulcerated intestines and bluut the sharpness of corroding humours. Forty or fifty drops of laudanum may be added: in which case, it will generally sup- ply the place of the Astringent Clyster. Turpentine Clyster.—-Take of common decoction, ten ounces; Venice turpentine, dissol-.ed with the yolk of an egg, halfanom.ee ; Florence oil, one ounce. Mix, them. _ This diuretic clyster is proper in obstructi-rn, of tbe urinary pas-ages, and in co- licky complaints proceeding from gravel. Vinegar Clyster.—This clyster is made by mixing three ounces of vinegar with five ef water-grueL It answers all the purposes of a common clyster, with the peculiar advantage of be- ing proper either in inflammatory or putrid disorders, especially in the latter. {Cf We think it unnecessary to give r..ore examples of this class of medicines, as ingredients adapted to any particular intention may be occasionally added to one or ether of the above forms. COLLTRIA, or EYE-WATERS. EYE-WATEUS have been inultipli--d without number, almost every ptnoti pre- tending to be possessed of some secret preparation for the cure of sore eyes. 1 have ex- amined many of them, and find that they are pretty much alike, the basis of most of them being either alum, vitriol or lead. Their effects evidently are, to brace and re- store the tone of the parts; hence they are principally of service in slight inflamma- tions ; and in that relaxed state of the parts which is induced by obstinateones. Camphor is commonly added to the.se compositions; but as it seldom iocoipOHMl. properly with tlie water, it can be of little use. Boles and other earthy substances, m thev do not dissolve in water, are likewise unfit for this purpose. Collyrium ofAlum.—-lake of akin, half a drachm ; agitate it well together with the white of one egg. This is the Collyrium of Birerius. It is used in inflammation of the eyes, to allay heat, and restrain the flux of humours. It must be spread upon linen, and applied tf the eyes, but should not bv kept on above three or four hours at a time. H Vitriolic Collyrium.—Take of white vitriol, half a drachm ; rose water, «lx ouncej. Dissolve the vitriol in the water, and filter the liquor. This, though simple, is perhaps equal in virtue to most of the celebrated collyris. It is an useful application in weak, watery, and inflamed eyes. Though tbe sligbtei inflammations will generally yield to it, yet in those of a more obstinate nature tbe as- sistance of bleeding and Qlistering«riU often be necessary. When a strong astringent is judged proper, a double or triple quantity ofthe vitri- •1 may be used. I hare seen a solution ef four times tbe strength of the above (ued wjypi manifest advantage* ■ APPENDIX. 355 Collyrium of Lead.—Take sugar of lead, and crude sal ammoniac, of eaeh fori* grains. Dissolve them in eight ounces of common water. Forty or fitly drops of laudanum may be occasionally added to this collyrium- Those who ch.isc may substitute in stead of this the collyrium of lead recommended by Goulard; which is made by putting twenty-five drops of his Extract of Lead to eight eunccK of water, and abiding a tea-spoonful of brandy. Indeed, common water and brandy, without any other addition, will in many ease* answer very well as a collyrium. An ounce of 'he latter may be added to five or six ounces of (lie former; and the eyes, if weak, bathe.! with it night and moraine, CONFECTIONS. COKF1-XTIONS containing above sixty ingredients are still to be found in some of the most reforuied dispensatories. As most oftheir intentions, however, may be more certainly,and as effectually answered by a few glasses of vine or grains of opium, we ■ball pass over this class of medicine very slightly. Japonic Confection.—■'!'.ke Japan earth, three ounces: tormentil root, nutmeg oliba- nam, of each two ounces ; opium dissolved in a sufficient quantily of Lisbon wine, a drachm and a half; simple syrup and conserve of roses, of each fourteen ounce*, Blix and make them into an electuary. This supplies thi place of the Diaacordium. The dose of this electuary is from u scruple to a drachm. CONSERVES and l'HESERVES. Every Apothecary's shop w as formerly so full of these preparations, that it might have passed for a confectioner's ware-house. They possess very few medicinal properties, and may rather beclasstd among sweetmeats than medicines. They are sometimes, how- ever, of use, (or reducing into boluses or pills some ofthe more ponderous powders^ as the preparations of iron, mercury, una tin. Conserves are compositions of fresh .eg. tables and sugar, beaten together into an uniform mass. In making these pre parations, the leaves of vegetables must be freed from tlieir stocks, the flowers from tlieir cups, and the yellow part of orange-peel taken oil" with a rasp. They are then to be pounded in a marble mortar, with a wooden pestle, into a smooth mass: after which thrice their weight of tine sugar is commonly addesi by degrees, and the beating continued till they are uniformly mixed ; but the conserve will be better if only twice its u eight of sugar be added. These who prepare large quantities of conserve geuerally reduce the vegetables to a pulp by the means of a mill, and afterwards beat them up with tlie sugar. Conserve of red roses*— Take a pound ot red rose buds, cleared of their heels ; beat them well in a mortar, and, adding by degrees two pounds of double-refined sugar, in powder, make a conserve. After the same manner are prepared the conserves of orange-peel, rosemary-flowcrfj sea wormwood, ofthe leaves of wood-sorrel, &c. The conserve of roses is one of the most agreeable and useful preparations belongi ng to this class. A drachm or two of it, dissolved in warm milk, is ordered to be given as a gentle re-stringent in weakness of the stomach,and likewise in phthisical coughs and ■pitting of blood. To have any considerable effects, however, it must be taken in larger quantities. Conserve of Sloes.—This may be made by boiling the sloes gently in water, be- ;,- log careful to take them out before they burst; afterwards expressing the juice, and beating it up with three times its weight of fine sugar. In relaxation of the uvtda and glands ofthe throat, this makes an excellent gargle, and Maybe used at discretion. Preserves are made by steeping or boiling fresh vegetables first in water, and after- wards in syrup, or a solution of sugar. 'Ihe subject is either preserved moist in the syrup, or taken out and dried, that the sugar may candy upon it. The lust is the most useful method.^. Candied Orange-peel.—Soak Seville orange-peel in several watAs till it loses its Witt' ness; then boil it in a solution of double-refined sugar in water, till it becomes lender aud transparent. Candied lemon-peel is prepared in the same manner. It is needless to add more of these preparations,as they belong rather to the an ofthe confectioner than that of the apothecary. DECOCTIONS. WATER readily extracts tlie gummy and saline parts of vegetables; and though its action is chiefly confined to these, yet the resinous and oily being intimately blended witli the gummy and saline, are in great part taken up along with them. Hence water*- decoctions and infusions of vegetables, constitute a large, and notuuuseful class of loeqiciaes. Although most vegetables yield their virtues to w ater, as well by infusion as decoction,yet the latter is often necessary, as it saves time, and does in a few minutes what tli-; other would require hours, and sometimes days,to effect. Tlie medicines of this class are all intended for immediate use. Decoction of AUhcea.—Take ofthe roots of n»an.h-mallows,moderatc!y dried, thrfe •Unces; raisins ofthe tun, one ounce, water three pints. 396 APPENDIX. Boil the ingredients in the water till one third of it is con turned; afterwards strain the decoction, and let it stand lor some time to settle. If the roots be thoroughly dried, they must be boiled till one hull'tin isattr becousumed. In coughs, and sharp dcfluctions upon the lungs, this decoction may be used for ordi- nan driids. Tlie Common Decoction,— Tike of camomile-flowers, one ounce; lider-flowers and sweet fennel seeds, of each half au ounce, water, two quarts. Boil them for a little, and then strain the decoction. A medicine equally good may be prepared by infusing the ingredients for some hours in boiling wat- r. s Thisdi coction is chiefly intended as the basis of clysters, to which other ingre- dients may )«■ occasionally added. It will likewise serve as a common fomenta- tion, spirit of wine or other things being added in such quantity as the case may require. Deroctun of Logu-ood.—Bo^ three ounces of the shav ings or chips of logwood; in four pints of water, till one half the liquor is wasted. Two or three ounces of simple cinna- mon water may be added to this decoction. In fluxes ofthe belly, where the stronger astringents are improper, a tea-cupful of this decoction may be taken with advantage three or tour times a-day. Decoction ofthe Bark.—Boil an ounce ofthe Peruvian bark, grossly powdered, in a pint and a half of water to one pint, then strain the decoction. If a tea-spoonful of the weak spirit of vitriol be added to this medicine, it rentiers it both more agreeable and effi- cacious. Compound Decoction of the Bark.—Take of Peruvian bark and Virginian snake-root, grossly powdered*eaeh three drachms. Boil them in a pint of water to one half. Te the strained Uquorkdd an ounce and a half of aromatic water. Sir John Pri^le'recommeiids this as a proper medicine towards the decline of malig- nant fevers, v. hen the pulse is low, the voice weak, and the head affected with a stupor but with httle delirium. This dose is four spoonsful every fourth or sixth hour. Decoction of Sarsaparilla.—Take of fresh sarsaparilla root, sliced and bruised, two •unees; shapings of guaiacum wood, one ounce. Boil onr a slow fire, in three quarts '; of water, to one; adding towards the end, half an ounce of sassafras wood, and three 'J| drachms of liquorice. Strain the decoction. This may either be employed as an assistant to a course of mercurial alterant, a, or .taken after the mercury has been osed for some time. It strengthens the noiuach, and restores flesh and vigour to habits emaciat d by the venereal diseases. It may also be taken in the rheumatism, and cutaneous disorders proceeding from foulness ofthe blood and juices. For all these intentions it is greatly preferable to the Deeoctijoi of ' This decoction may be taken, from a pint and a half to two quarts in the day. The following decoction is said to l>e similar to that used by Kennedy, in the can of the Tenereal diseases, and may supply the place of Lisbon diet drink. Take of Sarsaparilla, three ounces; liquorice and Mezerion root, u'ee'h li.ilf an ounce • shavings of guaiacum and sassafras wood, of each one ounce; cru '■■ a-.ti.-i.unj, lvnwdared.anoeMceiMid a half. Infuse these ingredients in eight pints i buiii-ig v>»- ter for twenty-ftr8r*irt%s, then boil them till one half of the water is con^i.i.d; after- wards strain the decoction. This decoction may be used in the same manner as the preceding. Decoction of Seneka.—Take ot'Seneka rattle-snake root one ounce; water a pint and a half. Boil to one pint, aid strain. This decoction is recommended in the pleurisy, dropsy, rheumatism, and some owu- nate disorders of tbe skin. The dose is two ounces, theee or four times a-day, or often- er. if the stomach will bear it. . . .. White Decoction.—Take ofthe purest chalk, in powder, two eunces; gam arabic, hall an ounce; water, three pints. Boil to one quart, and strain the decoction. This is a proper drink in acute diseases, attended with, or inclining to a looseness, and where acidities abound in the stomach or bowels. It is peculiarly proper lor cbdilrtu when afflicted with sourness of the stomach, and for persons who are subject to the heart-burn. It may be sweetened with sugar, as it is used,and two or thrie ounces ol simulecinnamen-water added to it. . „..„._. An ounce of powder, d chalk, mixed with two pints of water, will occasionally •upplT J th* nlace of this decoction, and also ofthe chalk julep. ""' DRAV'CHTS. TKISis a proper form for exhibiting such medicines as are intended to operate im- {! mediately, and which do not need to be frequently repeated, si purges, vomH*,«Hl a tw others which arc to be taken at one dose. Where a medicine requires to be used J-orJnyl,i'y<u.r tio. ,itf» better to make upalarger qauntity of itatoiice whieh saves iwi.ii iv.uble and expense. , !iri"r* * Daught.-Ta.ke of liquid laudanum, tw.ntr-fivc drops; simple cinnamon water i.n ounce ; eommoi. . nip, two drachms. Mix thetn. . in excessive pain, where bleeding is not necessary, and in great restlessness, this composing draught msy be taken and repeated occasionally. APPENDIX. *97 Diuretic Draught.-Take of the diuretic salt, two scruples ; syrup of poppies, two drachms ; simple einnamon-water, and common water, of each an ouuce. This draught is of service in an obstruction or deficiency of urine. Purging Draughts,—Take of manna, an ounce ; soluble tartar, or Rochel saU.from three to tour drachms. Dissolve in three ounces of boiling water ; to which add Ja- maica pepper water, half an ounce. -^ . As manna sometimes will not sit upon the- stomach, an ounce or ten drachms 01 ine bitter purging salts, dissolved in four ounces of water, may be taken instead of the above. Those who cannot take salts may use the following draught. I ake of jalap in powder, a scruple ; common water, an ounce; aromatic tincture six drachms. Hub the jalap with twice its weight of sugar, and add to it the other in- Siveatin" Draughts.—Take spirit of Mindererus, two ounces; salt of hartshorn, five grains ; simple cinnamon-water, and syrup of poppies, of each half an ounce. Make them into a draught. ..... . In recent colds and rheumatic complaints, this- draught is of service. To promote its effects, however, the patient ought to drink freely of warm water-gruel, or of some oilier weak diluting liquor. . , ___ Vomiting Drauphts.—Take of ipecacuanha in powder, a scruple; water an ounce, simple svrup a drachm. Mix them.—Persons who require a stronger vomit, may add to the above half a grain, or a grain, of emetic tarts r. »... - , » Those who do not chuse the powder, may take ten drachms of the ipecacuanha wine ; or half an ounce ofthe wine, and an equal quantity of the sjrup ol squills. ELECIUARILS. . ELECTUARIES are generally composed of the light- r powders, mixed with sy- rup, honey, conserve, or mucilage, into such a eonsjstence, that the powders may neither scparute by keeping, nor the mass prove too stift tor swallowing. .Ihey re- ceive chiefly the milder alterative medicines, and such as are not ungrateful to the ' ^As'triiiR -nt electuaries, and such as have pulps of fruit in them, should be prepared only in small quantities ; as astringent medicines lose their virtues by btmg kept in tl.i form, and the pulps of fruit are apt to ferment. . - . . - -p 1--..I the extraction of pulps it will be necessary to boil nnnpe fruits, and ripe ones it they are .hied, in a small quantity of water till they become soft. I he pulp is then to be pressed out through a strong hair sieve, or thin cloth,aud afterwards boiled to a due consistence, in an earthen vessel, overa gentle fire, taking care to prevent the matlet from burning by continually stirring it. The pulps of fruit that are both ripe and fresh, may be pressed out witliout any previous boiling. Lenitive Electuary.—Take of senna, in fine powder, eight ounces; conander seed, also in powder, four ounces ; pulp of tamarinds and Fnnch prunes, each a pound. Mix the pulps and powders together, and with a sufficient quantity of simple syrup, reduce the whole into an electuary. A tea-spoonful of this electuary, taken two or three times a-day, generally proves an greeable laxative. It likewise serves as a convenient vehicle for exhibiting mere ac H medicines, as jalaps, scammony. and such like. . This may supply the place of the electuary of Cassia. s,' Electuary for the Dysenten/ —Take of the Japonic confection, tv. o ounces ; Loca. i<tiui's balsam, one ounce ; rhubarb in powder, half an ounce; syrup of marsli-mallows, Bough to make an electuary. . .... It a often dangerous in dysrnteries to give opiates and astnngf-nts, without interposing fm jatives. The purgative is here joined with these ingredients, which renders this a very ssfe and useful medicine for the purposes expressed in the title. About the bulk of a nutmeg shohid be taken twice or thrice a-day, as the symptoms and constitution mav require. . Elettuaru for the Epilepsy.—Take of Peruvian bark in powder, an ounce ; ol pow- i!citdtin,aiid wild valerian root, each half an ounce ; simple syrup enough to make an C 9r? Mead directs a drachm of an electuary similar to this to be taken evening and morning, in the epilepsy, for the space of three months. It will be proper, bovvever. to iliseoniinue the use of it for a few days every now and then. I have added ilia pow- d. n d tin, because the epilepsy often proceeds from worms. «» . ElectuanttortkcConSrr.cea.-Takt of lenitive electuary, three ounce** jalap and rhubarb, in powder, of each two drachms; nitre, halt an ounce ; simple syr»p, enough; *l^ngthe^«?in«tion and tension of the urinary passages, which accompany a rirulcnt conorrh,. a.this cooling laxative may be used with advantage. The dSie is a drachm, or about the bulk ofa nutmeg, two or three tones a-day; more or less -«s mav be necessary to keep the body gently open. An el ciulry made of crrain of tartar and simple syrup will occanonally supply the P' After the1' nthiiumviuoii is gone off the follow.ng electuary my be wed i Jj I 508 APPENDIA. Take of lenitive electuary.two ounces; balsam of capiv,, one ouncr ; gum guniacura and rhubarb, in powder,of each two drachms; simple syrup, enough to make an elec- tuary. The dose is the same as of the preceding. Electuary ofthe Bark.—Take of Peruvian Bark, in powder, three ounces; cascarilla, half an ounce; syrup of ginger, enough to make an electuary. In the cure of obstinate intermiting fevers, the bark is assisted hy the cascarilla. In hectic habits, however, it will be better to leave out the cascarilla, and put three drachms of crude sal ammoniac in its stead. Electuary for the Piles.—Take flowers of sulphur, one ounce; cream of tartar, half an ounce ; ireacle,a sufficient quantity to form an electuary. A tea-spoonful of this may be taken three or four times a-day. Electuary for the Palsy.—Take of powdered mustard seed, and conserve of roses, each an ounce; syrup of ginger, enough to make an electuary. A tea-spoonful of this may be taken three or four times a-day. Electuary for tlie Rheumatism.—Take of conserve of roses, two ounces; cinnabar of antimony, levigated, an ounce and a half; gum guaiacum, in powder, an ounce ; syrup of ginger, a sufficient quantity to make an electuary. In obstinate rheumatisms, which are not accompanied with a fever, a tea-spoonful of this electuary may be taken twice a-day with considerable advantage. EMULSIONS. EMULSIONS, besides their use as medicines, are also proper vehicles for certain substances, which could not otherwise be conveniently taken in a liquid form. 1 Inn camphor, triturated with almonds, readily unites with water into an emulsion. Pure oils, balsams, raisins, and other similar substances, are likewise rendered miscible with water by tbe intervention of mucilages. Common Emulsion.—Take of sweet almonds, an ounce; bitter almonds, a drachm; water, two pints. Let the almonds be blanched, and beat up in a marble mortar adding the water, bj littleand little, so as to make an emulsion, afterwards let it be strained. Arabic Emulsion.—This is made in tbe same manner as the above, adding to the al- monds, while beating, two ounces and a half of the mucilage of gum arabic. Where soft cooling liquors are nccessiuy, these emulsions may be used as ordinary drink. Camphorated Emulsion.—Take of camphor, halfa drachm ; sweet almonds, halfa do- zen; white sugar, half au ounce ; mint-water, eight ounces. Grind the camphor and almonds well together, in a 9tone mortar, and add by degrees the mint-water; tli n strain the liquor, and dissolve in it the sugar. In fevers, and other disorders which require the use of camphor, a table-spoonful of this emulsion may be taken every two or three hours. Emulsion of Gum Ammoniac.—Take of gum ammoniac,two drachms; water, eight ounces. Grind the gum with the water poured upon it by little and little, till it is dis- solved. This emulsion is used for attenuating tough, viscid phlegm and promoting expecto- ration. In obstinate coughs, two ounces of the syrup of poppits may be added to it. The dose is two table-spoonsful three or four times a-day. Oily Emulsions-Take of soft water, six ounces; volatile aromatic spi.st, twodrachmi; Florence oil, an ounce; shake them well together, and add, of simple syrup, half an ounce. In recent colds and coughs, this emulsion is generally of service -, but if the cough proves obAinate, it will succeed better when made with the paregoric elixir of tbe Ed. inburgh Dispensatory, instead of the volatile aromatic spirit. A tablespoonfol of it mav be taken ever)- two or three hours. ' ": EXTRACTS. EXTRACTS are prepared by boiling tbe subject in water, and evaporating the strained decoction to a due consistence. By this process someof the more active part, of plants are freed from the us-, less, inui.solublc . arthy matti r, which makes the bu- ret-share of their bilk. Water, however, is not th. 'only menstruum used in the prepa- ration ol extracts ; sometimes it is joined wu|. spirit., and at other li.ne. rectified spirit alone is empljved for that purpose. ... Extracts arepreparid from a variety of different drugs, as the nark tcnuau, jalap, &c out rs 'l.ajyrequir-- a troublesome-aid teilious operation, it v. ill be more conven- ient fo a pii.-ite practitioner to purchase what lie needs of them from a proiessed drug- gist, thsr to prepare thuniiimsi If. Such ei iht -u- a- are gen. -rails used are inserted in ourbslofcuch drugs and ra.-uic.n -. as ire to be k p. for private practice. FOMENT rL UN.'.. FOMENTATIONS are generally imt -led ••'tbertocase i^ui.lr ■ taking cTtei.sionund snas'.i } °r '•' ' raceand roUivthe tone :ind vigour of iho,. par-. n .In :. tl, y are ap- ilie-d Tht f.rstol ikes.-intentions niay g. . ..-rally bu » osw-nd by warm wau-r, and tl u . coiul i-.v colJ.
