We shall tln-re- for--'- n join j f.-w of the most i.si-ti.1;.. d'ea..t! tbiucntatiuus, that people may hare it i> their power to laake use oi them if they chuse. APPENDIX. 3^9 Anodyne Fomentation— Take of white poppy-beads, two ounces; elder-flowers, half ai. .mnce, water, three pints. Boil till one pint is evaporated, and strain out the. K'p-or. 1 his fomentation, as its title expresses, is used for relievingacute pnin. Aromatic Fomentation.— 1 tike of Jamaica pepper, half an ounce; red wine, a pint floil them for a little, and then strain the liquor. , This is intended, not only as a topical application for external complaints, but also for relieving the internal parts. Pains ofthe bowels, wliich accompany dysenteries and diarrhoeas, flatulent colics, uneasiness of the stomach, and retchings to vomit, are frequently abated by fomenting the abdomen and region of the stomach with the warm Common Fomentation.--Take tops of wormwood and camomile-flowers, dried, of each two ounces; water two quarts. After a slight boiling, pour off the liquor. Brandy or spirit of wine may be added to this fomentation, in such quantity as the particular circumstances ofthe case shall require; but these are not always neces- sary. Emollient Fomentation.—This is the same as the common decoction. Strengthening Fomenttttion.—Tnke of oak bark, one ounce ; granate peel, "a'f»n ounce ; ilium, two drachms ; smith's forge water, three pints. Boil the water with the bark and p.el to the consumption of one-third: then strain the remaining decoction and dissolve alum in it. . This astringent liquor is employed as an external fomentation to weak parts: it may also be used internally. GARGLES. HOWEVER trifling this class of medicines may appear, they are by no means with- out tlieir use. They seldom indeed cure diseases, but they often alleviate very disa- greeable symptoms ; as parchedness of the mouth, foulness of die tongue and fauces, &c. they are peculiarly- useful in fevers and sore throats. In the latter, a gargle will sometimes remove the disorder ; and in the former few things are more refreshing ot agreeable to the patient, than to have his mouth frequently washed with seme solt detergent gargle. . .. , , . One advantage of these medicines is, that they are easily prepared. A little barley- water aud honey may be had any where, and if to these be added as much vinegar as will givethem an agreeable sharpness, they will make a very useful gargle lor soitening ind cleansing the mouth. Gargles have the best effect when injected with a syringe. Attenuating Gargle.—Take of water, six ounces ; honey, one ounce ; nitre, a drachm end a half. Mix them. . „ This cooling gargle may lie used either in the inflammatory quinsey, or in fevers, Sir cleaning the tongue and fauces. . „ ... Common Gargle.—Take of rose-water, six ounces ; syrup of clove, Julj-lioweps, nan an ounce ; spirit of vitriol, a sufficient quantity to give it an agreeable sharpness. Mix Ti.'is gargle, besides cleansing the tongue and fauces, acts as a gentle repellent, and will sometimes remove a slight quinsi.y. „ , Deicrg, ,.t Cur ',-._ Take ofthe emollient gargle, a pint; tincture of myrrh, an ounce; boncy. tv.nounces. Mix thein. . ., ,. When exulrerations require to be cleansed, or the excretion of tough viscid saliva pro- moted, this gargle will be of service. _ Emollient Gnmle__lake an ounce of Marshmallow roots,and two or three ngs. boil them in a 'quart of water till near one half of it be consumed, then strain out the "it Iu ounce of honey and half an ounce of spirit of sal-ammoniac be addssd to tbe jbove.it will then be an exceeding good attenuatinggwglf. This gargle is beneficial in fevers, where the tongue mid fauces are rough and parcii- sd. to soften these parts, and promote the discharge of saliva. 'Ihe learned and accurate Sir .lohn Pringle observes, that in tbe inflammatory quinsey, • or strangulation of the fauces, little benefit arises iron, the common gargles; tbatsucn as sreoran acid naturedo more barn, than good.by contracting ihe emunctories of the saliva ■lid mucus, and thickening those humours; that a decoction ot figs-.n milk "« water Ms „ ,„mr.,ry effect, especially if some sal-ammoniac be added, by which the sa livais made thinner, and the glands brought to seorete more freely? a circumstance alwavs conducive to the cure. INFVSIOSS. . VEGETABLES vield eearlv the same properties to water by .nfusion as tydecoe- , UOO; and though they may require a longer time to «ye out their vnjues in this way * yet it has several advantage* over t .e other; since bo.l,..g.s toundto q.ssipate he finer parts of many bitter and aromatic substances, w.ihout more fu.Iy extracting their "The^tK^Kew Dispensatory observes, that even from those vegetables which are weak in virtue rich infusions ma> be obtained, by returning the liquor upon fresh 409 APPENDIX. and that these loaded infusions are applicable to valuable purpeses in medicine, as tliry Contain in a small compass the finer, more subtle, and active principles of vegetables, in a form readily miscible with the fluids of the human body. Bitter I/fusion.—Take tops ofthe lesser centaury and camomile flowers, or each half an ounce; yellow rind of lemon and orange peel, carefully freed from ihe inner white part, of each two drachms. Cut them in small pieces, and infuse ihem in a quart of boiling water. For indigestion, weakness of the stomach, or want of appetite, a tea-cupful of tin infusion may be taken twice or thrice a-day. Infusion ofthe Bark.—To an ounce of the bark, in powder, add four or five table- spoonsful of brandy, and a pint of boiling- water. Let them infusefor two or three duvs. This is one of the best preparations of tbe bark tor weak stomachs. In disord.'ri where the corroborating virtues of that medicine are required, a tea-cupful of it may he taken two or three times a-day. Infunon ofCarduus—Infuse an ounce ofthe dried leave cf the cardous benc-dictm or blessed thistle, in a piut of common water, for six hours, without heat; then filler the liquor through paper. This light infusion maybe given with great benefit, in weakness ofthe stomach, vshere the common bitters do not agree. It may be flavoured at pleasure with china- mon, or other aromatic materials. Infusion of Linseed.—Take of linseed, two spoonsful; liquorice root, sliced, bslf an •mice: boiling water, three pints. Let them stand to infuse by the fire for some hours, and then strain off the liquor. If an ounce ofthe leaves of colt's foot be added to these ingredients, it will then be the Pectoral infusion. Both these are emollient mucilaginous liquors, and may be tak.ii with advantage as ordinary drink in difficulty of making water; and in coughs and ether complaints ofthe breast. Infusion of Roses.—Take ofred-roses, dried, half an ounce; boiling water, a quart; Yitnolic acid, commonly called oil of vitriol, half a drachm; loafsugar,t>n ounce. Infuse the rosea in the water four hours, in an' uuglazed earlht-n vessel: alien.anh pour in the acid, and having strained the liquor, add to it the si: i-ar. In an excessive flow ofthe menses, vomiting of blood, and otTher hrmorrharei, a lea- cupful of this gently astringent infusion may be taken every three or four hours. It likewise makes an exceeding good gargle. As the quantity of roses used here can have little or no effect, an equally valuable medicine may be prepared by mixing the acid and water without infusion. Infusion ofTamarinds andSenna.—Take of tamarinds, one ounce, semis, and crystals •f tartar, each two drachms. Let these ingredients be infused (bur or five hours in a pint of hiiilimr water, afterwards let the liquor be strained, and an ounce or two of tha aromatic tincture added to it. Persons who are easily purged may leave out either tht tamarinds or the crystals of tartar. This is an agreeable cooling purge. A tea-cupful maybe given every half hoar till it operates. This supplies the place ofthe Decoction ofTamarinds and Senna. Spanish Infusion.—Take of Spanish juice, cut into small pieces, an ounce; salt ortir- tar three drachms. Infuse in a quart of boiling water for a night, to the strained liquor add an ounce and an half of the syrup of poppies. In rec.nt colds, coughs, aud obstructions ofthe breast, a tea-cupful of this infusion may be taken »itli advantage, three or four times a-day. Infusion for the Palsy.—-Take of horse-radish root shaved, mustard-seed bruised, eacll i'otir ounces, outes rind of orange peel, one ounce. Infuse thein iu two quarts of boiling water, in a clos - vessi I for twenty-four hours. In paralytic complaints,a tea-cupful of this warm stimulating medicine may lie taken three orfour times a-day. It excites the actiou of the solids, proves diuretic, and il ili< iu:i. nt be kept warm, promotes perspiration. II two or three ounces ofthe dri.-d leaves of marsh-trefoil be used instead of mustard, it will make the Antiscorbutic Infusion. JULEPS. THE basis of julep? is generally common water, or some simple distilled water, with mi-.--third or one-fourth its quantity of distilled spirituous water, and as much sugar ot smuji as is sufficient to render the mixture agreeable. This is sharpened with vegetable or mineral acids, or impregnated will.- other medicines suitable to tbe intention. Ca ••phorated Jnle/>-—Takr of e-iupbor, one drachm; rectified spirit^ of wine, tea drops; double refilled sugar, hnlf'au ounce ; boiling distilled water, one pint. Rub the camphor first with the spirit of wine, then with the sugar; lastly, add the water by de- grees, ami strain the liquor- In hysteric.I and other complaints where camphor is proper, thisjnlcp may be taken in the dose of :t spoonful or two as often as the stomach will b -ar it. C ordinl Julep— Take of simple cinnamon- water, four ounces; Jamaica pepner-wat^r, ivim oiiucis; vo'atile aromatic spirit, and compound spirit of lavender, of each two drachms; syrup of orange-peel, an ounce. Mix them. This is given in the dose of two spoonsful three or four times a-day, in disorders ac- companied with giant weakness and depression of spirit*. APPF.vniv. 401 Exprrtorating Ji.lep.—Take ofthe emulsion of gum ammonia^ six ounces; svrup of .quill-, two ounces. Mi\ them. In coughs, asthmas, and obstructions ofthe breast, two table-spoonsful of this julep may be- taken every three or four hours. MuskJuleJi.—Hub halfa drachm of musk well together with half an ounce of sugar, and add to it gradually, of simple cinnamon and peppermint water, each two ounces; of the volatile aromatic spirit, two drachms. In the low state of nervous fevers, hiecuping, convulsions, and other spasmodic affec- tions, two table-spoonsful of this julep may be taken every two or three hours. Saline Julep.— Dissolve two drachms of salt of tartar in three ounces of fresh lemon toice, strained ; when the effervescence is over, add, of mint-water, and common water, each two ounces ; of simple syrup, one ounce. This removes sickness at the stomach, relieves vomiting, promotes perspiration, and maybe of somi service in fevers, especially ofthe inflammatory kind. Voni ■ir,: • j.drt;,—Dissolve four grains of emetic tartar in eight ounces of water, and add to it hall au ou:ic> ofthe syrup of clove July-flowers. j In the beginning of fevers, where there is no topical inflammation, this julep may be given in tlu-^lese of one table-spoonful every quarter of an hour till it operates. Anti- monul vomits serve not only to evacuate the contents of the stomach but likewise to promote the different excretions. Hence they are found in fevers to have nearly the same effect as Dr. James' Powder. MIXTURES. A MIXTURE differs from a julep in this respect, that it receives into its composition notonly salts, extracts and other substances dissoluble in water, but also earths, pow- ders,aud such substances as cannot be dissolved. A mixture is seldom either an elegant or agreeable medicine. It is nevertheless necessary. Many persons can take a mixture, who are not able to swallow a bolus or an electuary: besides, there are medicines which act better in this than in any other form. Astringent Mixture.—Taka simple cinnamon water and common water, of each three ounces ; spirituous cinnamon water, an ounce and a half; Japonic confection, half an ounce. Mix them. In dysenteries which arc not of long standing, after the necessary evacuations, a spoonful or two ofthis mixture may be taken every four hours, interposing every se- cond or third day a dose of rhubarb. Diuretic Mixture.—Take of mint-wat-r, five ounces; vinegar of squills, six drachms; sweet spirit of nitre, half an ounce; syrup of ginger, au ounce and an half. Mix them. In obstructions ofthe urinary passages, two spoonsful of this mixture may be taken twice or thrice a-day. Laxative absorbent Mixture.—'Rnh one drachm of magnesia alba in a mortar with ten or twelve grains ofthe best Turkey rhubarb, and add to them three ounces of com- mon water ; simple cinnamon water, and syrup of sugar, of each one ounce. ' As most diseases ol infants are accompanied with acidities, this mixture may either be civ-eii with a view to correct these, or to open the body. A table spoonful may betaken for a dose, and repeated three times a-day. To a very young child half a spoonful wiU ' bv sufficient. When the mixture is intended to purge, the dose may either be increased, or the quan- tily of rhubarb doubled. This is one of the most generally useful medicines for children with which I am ac- quainted. Saline Mixture—Dissolve a drachm of the salt of tartar in four ounces of boiling wa- t. r; and when cold, drop into it spirit of vitriol till the effervccenceceases; then add ot peppermint-water, two ounces, simple Syrup, one ounce. Where fresh lemons cannot be had, this mixture r.iay occasionally supply the place ofthe saline julep. Squill Mixture.—Take of simple cinnamon water, five ounces; vinegar of squills one ounce, «yrup of inarshiuallow.s,an ounce and au half. Mix them. This niixtuie, by promoting expectoration, and the secretion of urine, proves ser- viceable in asthmatic and dropsical habits. A table spoonful of it may be taken fre- quently. OINTMENTS, LINIMENTS, AND CERATES. NOTWITHSTANDING the extravagant encomiums which have been bestowed on different prep.ir.itioiis ofthis kind, v. itli regard to tlieir efficacy ir. the cure of wounds Mires, etc. it is beyond adoubt,that the most proper application to a grien wound is! Ury lint. But though ointments do not heal wounds and sores, yet they serve to de- tend them from the external air, and to retain such substances as may be necessary for . drying, deterging, destroying proud flesh,and such like. For these purposes, however it will besufil.iei.tto insert only a few ofthe most simple forms,as ingredients ofa! wore active nature can occasionally be added to them. Tetloio Banticum Oitttment.—Take of jellow wax, white resin, and frankincense each u quarter of a pound ; melt them together over a gentle fire; then add of hog's lard' jiepand.oni pound. Strain the ointment while warm. L 1 2 402 APPENDIX. TT.W MiimfB. n employed for cleansing and healing wounds and ulcers. Ointment of Calamine.—Take of olive oil, a pint and a half; white wax, anil calamine stone levigated, of each halfa pound. Let the calamine stone, reduced into a fine powder, be rubbed with some part of the oil, and afterwards added to the rest of the oil and wax, previously melted together, continually stirring them till quite cold. This ointment, which is commonly known by the name of Turner's Cerate, is an ex- ceeding good application in burns and excoriations from whatever cause. Emollient Ointment.—Take of palm oil, two pounds; olive oil.a pint and half; yet low wax, halfa pound; Venice turpentine, a quarter of a pound. Melt the wax in the oils over a gentle fire; then mix in the turpentine, and strain the ointment. This supplies the place of AllhceaOintment. It may be used for anointing inflamed parts, Sec. Eye Ointment.—Take of begs' lard prepared, four ounces; white wax, two drachms -, tutty prepared, one ounce; melt the wax with the lard over a gentle fire, and then sprinkle in the tutty, continually stirring them till the ointment is cold. This ointment will be more efficacious, an.lof abetter consist, ncc, if two or three ilrachms of camphor be rubbed up with a little oil, and intimately mixed with it. Another—Take of'camphor,and calamine stone levigated, eaeh six drachms; verdi- grease well prepared, of each two drachms, hogs' lard and mutton suet prepared, of each two ounces. Rub the camphor well with the powder; afterwards mix in the lard and suet, continuing the triture till they be perfectly united. This ointment has been long hi esteem for diseases of the eyes. It ought, however, to be used with caution, when the eyes are much inflamed or very tender. I-suc Ointment.—Mix half an ounce of Spanish Hies, finely powdered, in six ounces of yellow basilicum ointment. This ointment is chiefly intended fordressing blisters, in order to keep them open da- ring pleasure. Ointment of Lead.—Take of olive oil halfa pint; white wax, two ounces; sugar of lead, three drachms. Let the sugar of lead, reduced into a fine powder, be rubbed up with some part ofthe oil, and afterwards added to the other ingredients, previously milt- ed together, continually stirring them till quite cold. This cooling and gently astringent ointment may be used in all cases where the in- tention is to dry and skin over the part, as in scalding, &c. Mercurial Ointment.—Take of quicksilver, two ounces ; hogs' lard, three ounces; mutton suet, one ounce. Rubthe quicksilver with an ounce ofthe hogs' lard in a warm mortar, till the globules be perfectly extinguished ; then rubit up with tbe rest of the lard and suet, previously melted together. Tlie principal intention of this ointment is to convey mercury into the body by being rubbed upon the skin. Ointment of Sulphur.—Take oThvp' lard prepared, four ounces; flowers of sulphur, an ounce and an half; crude sal ammoniac, two drachms, essence- of lemon, tenor twelve drops. Make them into an ointment. This ointment,rubbed upon the parts affected, will generally cure the itch. It is both the safest and best application for that purpose, and, when made in this way, has no disagreeable smell. WhiteOintment.—-Take of olive oil, one pint; white wax and spermaceti, of each Aree ounces. Melt them with agentle heat, and keep them constaatly and briskly stir- ring together, till quite cold. _ If two drachms of camphor» previously rubbed with a small quantity of oil, be added so the above, it will make the White Camphorated Ointment. Liniment Ointment.—Take equal parts of Florence oil, or fresh drawn linseed oil, and lime-water: shake them well together in a wide mouthed bottle, so as to form a thuiuent. This is found to be an exceeding proper application for recent scalds or burns. It may either be spread upon a cloth, or the parts affected may be anointed with it twice ot thrice a-day. ... , ■ i White Liniment.—This is made in the same manner as the white ointment, two-tlunis of the wax being left out. ■ This liniment may be applied in cases of excoriation, where on account or the large- ness ofthe surface, the ointments with lead or calamine might be improper. Liniment for tlie Piles.—Take of emollient ointment, two ounces, liquid laudanum, ball'an ounce. Mix these ingredients with the yolk of an egg, and work them well to- Volatile Liniment.—Take of Florence oil, an ounce; spirit of hartshorn, half an ounce. Shake them together. ...... . This liniment, made with equal parts ofthe sp^ t and oil will be more efficacious, Where the patient's skin is able to bear it. enedt ing, either toYessen <ff carry cff"|B» swiBpWt. The twin of this observation I have often experienced. APPENDIX. 403 Camphorated Oil.—Rub an ounce or camphor with two ouuces of Florence oil, in a mortar, till the camphor be entirely dissolved. This antispasmodic lininv-nt may bu used in obstinate rheumatisms, and in some other cusi s accompanied vs itli extreme pain and tension of the parts. PILLS. MEDICINES which opt.rate in a small dose, and whose disagreeable taste, or smell, makes it n.-e. «,ary that they should be concealed from th>- palate, are ni03t corn mod i- ously (Ahihiied in this form. No- medicine, however, that is intended to operate quick- ly, oie;ln to be made into pills, as they often lie for a considerable time on the stomach before tie y are dissolved, so as to produce any effect. As tie- ingredients which enter the composition of pills arc generally so contrived, that one pill of an ordinary size may contain about five grains of the compound, in mentioning the dose we shall only specify the number of pills to be taken, as one, two, three, Ike. Composing Pill.—Take of purified opium, ten grains; Castile soap, halfa drachm. Beat tlieni together, aud form the whole into twenty-pills. When a quieting draught will not sit upon tlie stomach, one, two or three of these pills may betaken, as occasion requires. Fietid Pill—'lake of asafoetida, half an ounce ; simple syrup as much as is necessary to form it into pills. In hysteric complaints, four or five pills, of an ordinary size, may be taken twice or thrice a-day. They may likewise be of service to persons afflicted with the asthma. , When it is necessary to keep the body open, a proper quantity of rhubarb, aloes, or jalap, may occasionally be added to tbe above mass. Hemlock Pill.—Take any quantity of the extract of Hemlock, and adding to it about n fifth part its weight ofthe powder of the dried leaves, form it into pills of the ordinary size. The extract of hemlock may be taken from one grain to several drachms in the day. The best method, however, of using these pills, is to begin with one or two, and to in- crease the dose gradually, as far as the patient can bear them, without aay remarkable degree of stupor or giddiness. Mercurial Pi/l.—Take of purified quicksilver and honey, each half an ounce. Rub them together in a mortar, till the globules of mercury are perfectly extinguished; then add of Castile soap, two drachms; powdered liquorice, or crumb of bread, a sufficient quantity to give tlie mass a proper consistence for pills. When stronger mercurial pills are wanted, the quantity of quicksilver may be dou- bled. The dose of these pills is different, according to the intention with which they are Siren. As an alterant, two or three may be taken daily. To raise a salivation, four or ve will be necessary. _ Equal parts of the above pill and powdered rhubarb made into a mass, with a suffi- cient quantity of simple syrup, w ill make a Mercurial purging pill. Mercurial Sublimate Pill.—Dissolve fifteen grains ofthe corrosive sublimate of mercu- ry in two drachms of the saturated solution ot crude sal-ammoniac, and make it into a paste, in a glass mortar, with a sufficient quantity of the crumb of bread. This mass must be formed into one hundred and twenty-pills. This pill, wliich is tbe most agreeable form of exhibiting the sublimate, has been Biund efficacious, not only in curing the venereal disease, but also in killing and expel- ling woi'ms, after other powerful medicines had failed.* For the venereal disease, four of these pills may be taken twice a-day, as an alterant three, and for worms two. Piummer's Pill.—Take of calomel, or sweet mercury, and precipitated sulphur and antimony,each three drachms; extract of liquorice, two drachms. Rub the sulphur anil mercury well together; afterwards add the extract, and with a sufficieut quantity of tbe mucilage of gum-arabic make them into pills. This pill has been founda powerful,yet safe-, alterativein obstinatecutaneous disor- ders ; and has completed a cure after salivation had failed. In venereal citses it has like- wise produced excellent effects. Two or three pills of an ordinary size may be taken night and morning, the patient keeping moderately warm, and drinking after each dose • draught of decoction ofthe woods, or of sarsaparilla. Purg'ng Pills.—lake of siiccotorine aloes, and Castile soap, each two draebms ; of simple syrup,a sufficient quantity to make them into pills. Four or five of ihesr- pills will generally prove a sufficient purge. For keeping the bi- dy gent l\ open, «ne may be taken night and morning. They are reckoned both deob- itruem .ind siomachie, and will be found to answer all the purposes of Dr. Anderson': pills, the piiicipal ingredient of which is aloes. Where :•■ luetic purges are improper, the following pills may be used : Take extract or jalap, vitr'.olatea tartar, of each two drachms; syrup of ginger, as much as will make' them a proper consistence for pills. • See a ;>■ .-/'-'• on thi s .-iifyterin the Edinburgh Physical and Literary Essays, by the in- f- t tut L." A '■'» G'u. i/t'itV. 404 APPl.NDlA These pills may be taken in tin! same quantity as the above. Pill for the Jaundice.—Take of Castile soap, succotorin. aloes, and rhubarb, of each' one drachm. Make them into pills with a sufficient quantity of syrup or mucilage, These pills, as their title express, are chiefly intended for the jaundice, which, wild the assistance of proper diet, they will often cure. Five or six of them may lie taken twice a-day, more or less, as is necessary to ke.p tbe body open. It will be proper, however, during their use, to interpose a vomit of ipecacuanha or tartar cnetic. Stomachic Pill.—Take extract of gentian, two drachms ; povsil, red rhubarb and vitria. lated tartar, of each one drachm; oil of mint, thirty drops; simple svrup, a sufficient quantity. Three or four of these pills may be taken twice a-day, for invigorating the stomach, and keeping the body gently open. Squill Pills.—Take powder of dried squills, a drachm and n hulf; gumammoniic, and cardamom seeds, in powder, of each three drachms; simple svrup, a sufficient quantity. In dropsical and asthmatic complaints, two or three of these pills m.iv be taken twice a-day, or oftener, if the stomach will bear them. Strengthening Pills.—Take soft extract ofthe bark, and salt of steel, each a drachm. Make into pills. In disorders arising from excessive debility, or relaxation of the solids, as the chlorosis, or green sickness, two of these pills may he taken three times a-day. PLASTERS. PLASTERS ought to be of a different consistence, accoiding to the purposes for which they are intended. Such as are to be applied to tbe breasts or stumueh ought to be soft and yielding; while those designed to the limbs should be firm tind adhesive. It has been supposed, that plasters might be impregnated with the virtues of different vegetables, by boiling the recent vegetable with the oil employed for the composition of the plaster; but this treatment does not communicate to the oils any valuable qualities. The calces of lead boiled with oils unite with thein into a plaster ofa proper consis- tence, which make the basis of several other plasters. In boiling these composition., a quantity of hot water must be added from time to time to prevent tbe plaster from burning or growing black. This, however, should be done with care, l.-it it cause the matter to explode. Common Plaster.—Take of common olive oil, six pints; litharge, reduced to a fine powder, two pounds and a half. Boil the litharge and oil together over a gentle fire, continually stirring them, and keepingalwavs about half» gallon ol'water in the vessel: after they have boiled about three hours, a little ofthe plaster may be taken out and put into cold water, to try if it be of a proper consistence: when that is the case, the whole may be suffered to cool, and the water well pressed out of it with the hands. This plaster is generally applied in slight wounds and excoriations ofthe skin. It keeps the part soft and warm, and defends it from the air, which is all that is necessary in such cases. Its principal use, however, is to serve as a basis for other plasters. Adhesive Plaster.—Take of the common plaster, half a pound, of Burgundy pitch, a quarter ofa pound. Melt them togi thi r. This plaster is principally used for keeping on other dressings. Anodyne Plaster.—Melt an ounce of adhesive plaster, and, when it is cooling, mix with it a drachm of powdered opium, and the same quantity of camphor, pr.vic.usly rubbed up with a little oil. . This plaster generally gives ease in acute pains especially ol the i>-rvoits kind. hUsiering Plaster.—Take of Venice turpentine, six ounces; yellow wax, two ounces; Spanish flies in fine powder, three ouuees ; powdered mustard, one '.'nice. Melt tin: wax. and while it is warm, add to it the turpentine, taking care not to evaporate it by too much teat. After the turpeulinc and wax are sufficiently incorporated, sprinkle in the powders, continually stirring the mass till it be cold. Though this plaster is made in a variety of ways, one seldom meets with it ofa proper consistence. When compeunded with oils and other greasy substances, its effects are blunted, and it is apt to run; while pitch and resin render it too hard and vtijr inconvenient. .... . ,.,,•• ,v. When the blistering plaster is not at hand,its place may be supplied by mixing * itn any soft ointment a sufficient quantity of powdered flics, or by forming them into a pasts with flour and vinegar. . Gum Plaster.—Take of the common plaster, four pounds; gum ammoniac and gal'*: num, strained, of each halfa pound. Melt them together, and add, of Venice turpeu- tine, six ounces. This plaster is used as a digestive, and likewise for discussing indolent tumours. J.lercurial Plaster.—Take of common plaster, one pound ; of gum ammoniac, stn.tK d. halfa pound. Melt thein together,and when cooling, add eight ouuees of quicksilver, previously extinguished by thtar*, with three ounces of hog's lard. This plasti r is recommended in pains ofthe limbs arising from a venereal cause. Indurations of the glands, and other violent tumours, are likew ise found sometimes to Stomach Plaster.—Take of gum plaster, hulf a pound, camphorated oil, an ounce »kd APPENDIX. 406 a half; black pepper, or capsicum, where it can be had, ene ounce. Melt the plaster, and mix with it the oil; then sprinkle in the pepper, previously reduced to a fane powder. „ An ounce or two ofthis plaster, spread on soft leather, and applied to the region ot the stomach, will be of service, in flatulencies arising from hysteric ami hypochondriac affections. A little of the expressed oil of mace, or a few drops of the essential od Of mint, may lie' rubbed upon it before it is applied. This may supply the place ofthe Antihysteric Plaster. Warm Plaster.—Take of guin plaster, one ounce; blistering plaster, two drachms. Melt them together over n gentle (ii e. . This plaster is useful in tbe sciatica and other fixed pains ofthe rheumatic kind : it ought, how ever, to be worn for some time, at.d to be renewed at least once a week. It this is found to blister the part, which is some limes the ease, it must be made with a smaller proportion ofthe blistering plaster. Wax Plaster.—Take of yellow wax, one pound; white resin, half a pound ; mutton sue t, three quarters ofa pound. Meli thein together. 1 bis is generally used instead or the Melilvt Plaster. It is a proper application after blisters,and in other cases where a gentle digestive is necessary. POWDERS. THIS is one ofthe most simple forms in which medicine can be administered. Many medicinal substances, however, cannot be reduced into powder, and others are too disa- greeable to be taken in this form. ...... Tbe lighter powders may be mixed in any agreeable tlun liquor, as tea or water gru- el. The more ponderous will require a more consistent vehicle, as syrup, conserve, jelly, or honev. .... . . . Gums, and other substances which are difficult to powder, should be pcunded along with tbe drier ones; but those which are too dry, especially aroinatics, ought to be sprinkled during their pulverisation, with afew dropsoi'any proper water. Aromatic powder> are to be prepared only in small quantities at a time, and kept in glass vessels closely stopped. Indeed no powders ought to be exposed to the air or kept too long, otherwise their virtues will be in a great measure desirced. As'rngent Poirder.—Take of alum and japan earth,each two drachms: Pound them together, and divide-the whole into fan or twelve doses. In an immediate flow ofthe menses, and other hemorrhages, one of these powders ■ay be taken erery hour, or every half hour, if the discharge i* violent. Powder of Bote.—Take of bole armenic. or French bole, two ounces; cinnamon, one ounce ; tormrntil root and guin arabic. of each six drachms ; long pepper, one drnehm. Let all these ingredients be reduced into a powder. This warm, glutinous, astringent powder, is given in fluxes, and other disorders where medicines of that class are uec ssary, in the d.se ofa scruple, or half a drachm. If a drachm or opium be added, it will make the powder of bole with o/>ium, which is a medicine of considerable efficacy. 11 may be taken in the same quantity as the for- mer, but not above tw ice or thrice a-day. Cmrminatiye Pmeder.—take of coriander-seed, half an ounce; ginger, one drachm ; nutmegs, halfa drachm, fine sugar, a drachm and a half. Reduce them into powder for twelve doses. ..... This powder is employed for expelling flatulencies arising from indigestion, particu- larly those to w bich hysteric and hypochondriac persons an- so liable. It may likewise begiveu in small quantities to children in their food, when troubled with gripes. Diuretic Poirder.—Take of gum arabic, four ounces ; purilied nitre, one ounce. Pound them together, and divide the whole into twenty-four doses. During the first siage ofthe vencr. al diseases, one of these cooling powders may be taken tlire. times a .lav, with considerable advantage. Ai unntic Opening Powder.—Tak* of tin best Turkey rhubarb, cinnamon, and fine Sugar, each two drachms. Let the ingredients be pounded, and afterwards mixed well together. When flatulency is accompanied with costiveness, a tea-spoonful of this powder may be taken once or twice a-day, according to circumstances. Saline I '•■entive poivder.— lake of soluble tartar, and cream of tartar, each one drachm. 1 urified nitre, halfa drachm. Make them into a powder. In fevers and other inflammatory disorders, where it is necessary to keep the body gently open, one of these cooling laxative pow ders, may be taken in a little grnel, and repealed occasionally. Steel Ponder.—T«ke filings of steel, and loaf sugar, of each two ounces; ginger, iwodraetnm. Pound them together. ... In obstructions ofthe menses, and other cases where steel is proper, a tea spoonful ofthis pov.der may be t ken twice a-d.iy,and washed down with a little wine or water. Stidoii/1- Powder.— 1..I-.' purified nitre and vitriolated tartar, of each half anounee; opium and ipecacuanha, of each one druchm. Mi\ the ingredients and reduce them to a tine powder. „ _ , _ _ . _- , . .- 'I bis is g. nrrally known by the name of Dover s Powder. It is a powerlul sudorific In obstinate rlicuiintisms, and other cases where it is necessary to excite ft copious iweat, this pow.lt. ma \ be administered iu the dose ofa scruple or half a drachm. 406 APPENDIX. Some patients will require two scruple.. It ought to be accompanied with the plentiful use of some warm diluting liquor. Worm Powders.— Take of tin reduced into a fine powder, an ounce ; .'Kihiop's mine ral, two drachms. Mix them well to-. ther. and divide tbe w hole into six dose.. One of tliese powders may be taken in a little syrup, honey, or treacle, twice a-day. After thpy have been all used, the following anthelmintic purge may be proper. Pti'-ging Worm powdei:—Take of post dered rhubarb a scruple ; scnrumony and Cal- omel, of each five grains. Rub them together in a uiortar for one dose. For children, the above doses must be lessened, according to their are. If the powderof tin be given alone, its dose may be considerably increased. The late Dr. Alston gave it to the 11 mount of two ounces in three days, and snvs, .rlu-n thus ad- ministered, that it proved an egregious anthelmintic. He purged his patients both be- fore they took the powder and afterwards. Powderfor the Tape-Worm.—Early in the morning the patient is to take in any liquid, two or three drachms according to his age and constitution, ofthe root ol tlie male fern reduced into a fine powder. About two hours afterwards he is to lake of calomel and resin of scammony, eacb ten grains ; gum gambougc, six grains. Tin se ingredients must be finely powdered and given in a little syrup, honey, treacle, or any thing that is most agreeable to the patient, lie is then to walk gently about, now and then drinking a dish of green tea, till the worm is passed. If the powder of tin- fern produces nausea, or sickness it may be removed by sucking the juice of an orange or lemon. This medicine, which had been long kept a secret abroad for tbe cure ofthe tape- worm, was some time ago purchased by the French King, and made public for the ben- efit of mankind. Not having bad an opportunity of trying it, I can sav nothing from ex- perience concerning its efficacy. It seems, however, from its ingredients, to be an ac- tive medicine, and ought to be taken with care. The dose here prescribed is sufficient for the strongest patieut; it must, therefore, be reduced according to the age and con- stitution. SYRUPS. SYRUPS were some time ago looked upon as medicines of considerable value. They are at present, however, regarded chiefly as vehicles for medicines of greater effi- cacy, and are used for sweetening draughts, juleps, or mixtures ; and reducing the lighter powders into boluses, pills, and electuaries. As all these purposes may be an- swered by the simple syrup alone, there is link occasion for any other; especially as they are seldom found but in a stato of fermentation ; and as the dost of any medicine given in this form is very uncertain. Persons who serve the public must keep what. ever their customers call for; bnt to the private practitioner nine-tenths of ihe sv rups usually kept in the shops are unnecessary. Simple Syrup.—Is made by dissolving in water, either with or without heat, about double its weight of fine sugar. If twenty-five drops of laudanum be added loan ounce ofthe simple syrup, it will supply the'place of diacodium/or the syrup of poppies, and will be found a more sals and certain medicine. The lubricating virtues of the syrup of marshmallows, may likewise be applied, by adding to the common syrup a sufficient quantity of mucilage of gum arabic. Those wbochusc to preserve the juice of lemons in form of syrup, may dissolve in it, by tbe heat ofa warm bath, ncttrh double its weight of fine sugar. '1 he juir. oo^l.t to be pre viously strained, and su fl. red to stand till it settles. The syrup of ginger is sometimes of use as a warm vehicle for giving medicines to persons afflicted with flatulency. It may be made by infusing two ounces of bruised gingir in two piiitsof boiling water for twenty-four hours. Afterthe liquor lim be.n strained, and ie»s aiood to settle for some time, it may be poured off, mid a liulc mom ttian double its weight of fine powdered sugar dissolved in it. TINCTURES, ELIXIRS, ere. RECTI7-I F.D spirit is the direct menstruum ofthe resins and essentialoils of vegeta- bles, and totally extracts tin se active principles from sundry substances, which yield them *o water, either not at all or only in part. It dissolves likew is- those parts of animal substances in which their peculiar smells and tastes reside. Ii. no the tinctures prt pared with rectified spirits ibi-m an ustml and elegant class ol medicines, possessing many ofthe most essential virtues of simples, without being logged w it), their inert or useless parts. Water, however, being the prop"r menstruum of the gum my, saline, and saccharine pru-ts of medical substances, it will be necessary, in the preparation of <• veral tinctures, to make use of a weak spirit, ora composition of rectified spirit and water. * Aromatic Tincture.—Infuse two ounces of Jamaica pepper in two pints of brain!). without heat, for a few days ; then strain off the tincture. This simple tincture will sufficiently answe-all the intentions of the more costly pre- parations ot Ibis kind. It is rather too hot to be taken by itself; but is very proper for mixing with such medicines as might otherwise prove too cold for the stomach. Compound Tincture ofthe Bark.—Take of Peruvian bark, two ounces; Seville oraf'i ■ peel and cinnamon, of each Italian ounc. Let the berk be powdered, and the oib. • ingredients bruised; then infuse tbe wh .!■ in a pint and a half of Brandy, for fire <•- six days, in a close vessel; afterwards >•...,,u off the tincture. <m' ■ APi-i ND1X. *07 The tincture is not only beneficial in intermitting fevers, but also in the slow, ner- vous, and putrid kinds, especially towards their decline. The dose is from one drachm to three or four, every fifth or sixth hour. It may be given in any suitable liquor, and occasionally sharpened with a few drops of the spirit of vitriol. Volatile Foetid Tincture.—Infuse two ounces of asafcetida in one pint of volatile aro- matic spirit, for eight days, in a close bottle, frequently shaking it. Then strain the tincture. This medicine is beneficial in hysteric disorders, especially when attended with low. ness of spirits, and faintings. A tea-spoonful of it may be taken in a glass of wine, or a cup of penny-royal lea. Volatile Tincture tf Gum Guaiacum.—Takeof gum guaiacum,. four ounces ; volatile aromatic spirit, a pint. Infuse without heat, in a vessel well stopped for a few days ; then strain oil'the tincture*. In the rheumatic complaints,a tea-spoonful of this tincture may be taken in a cup of the infusion of water trefoil, twice or thrice a-day. Tincture of Black Hellebore.—Infuse two ounces of the roots of black hellebore, bruis- ed, in a pint of proof spirit, for seven or eight days ; then filter the tincture through papc r. A scruple of cochineal may be infused along with the roots, to give the tincture a colour. In obstructions of tbe menses, a tc« «pannful of this tincture may be taken in a cup of camomile or pcnnj-roynl tea twice a-day. Astringent Tincture—Digest two ounces of gum kino, in a pint and a half of brandy, for eight days; afterwaids strain it for use. This tincturelhough not generally known, isa good astringent medicine. With this view, an ounce, or more, of it may lie taken three or four limes a-day. 'Tincture of Myirhand Aloes.—Take of gum myrrh, an ounce and a half; hepatic aloes, an ounce. Let tlieni be reduced to a powder, aud infused in two pints of rectified spirits, for six days, in a gentle heat; then strain the tincture. This is principally used by surgeons for cleansing foul ulcers, and restraining the pro- gress of gangn m s. It is also, by some, recommended as a proper application to green wounds. Tincture of Opium or Liquid Laudanum.—Take of crude opium, two ounces ; spi- rituous aromatic water and mountain w ine, each ten ounces. Dissolve the opium, sliced, in the wine, with a gentle heat,frequently stirring it; afterwaids add the spi- rit, and strain off the tincture. As twenty-five drops of this tincture contain about a grain of opium, tbe common dose may be from twenty to thirty drops. Sacred Tincture, or Tincture oj liirea Picra.—Take of suceotorine aloes in powder, one ounce ; Virginian snake-root aud ginger, of each two drachms. Infuse in a pint of mountain wine, and halfa pint of Drandy, for a week, frequently shaking the bottle, then strain off the tincture. This is a safe nnd useful purge for persons ofa languid and phlegmatic habit; but is thought to have better effects, taken in so.all doses as a laxative. The dose, as a purge, is from one to two ounces. Compound Tincture of Senna.— Take of senna, one ounce ; jalap, coriander seeds, and cream of tartar,of each half an ounce. Infuse1 them in a pint and a half of French brandy for a week ; then strain the tincture, and add to it four ounces of fine sugar. This is an agreeable purge, and answers all the purposes ot the Elixir salutis, and of Dafftjs Elixir. The dose is from one to two or three- ounces. Tinctui e of Spanish Flies.— Take of Spanish fl>s. reduced to a fine powder, two oun- ces ; spirit of wine, one pint. Infuse for two or vhrtedays ; then strain oil the tincture. This is intended as an acrid stiiiiiila.it tor external use. Parts a dieted with the pal- sy Or chronic rheumatism may be frequently rubbed with it. Tincture of the BaU-ai>: ofTolu.—'lake of the llalsani of Tolu, an ounce and a half; rectified spirit of wine, a pint. Infuse iu a gentle beat until the balsam is dissolved, then strain the tincture. This tincture possesses all the virtues ofthe balsam. In coughs, and other complaints ol' the breust, a tea-spoonful or two of il may be taken in a bit ol loaf sugar. But the best way of using it is in syrup. An ounce of iho unc.uiv, pioprly mixed with two pounds of simple syrup, will make what is commonly cailes! the Balsamic syrup. Tincture of Rhubarb.—-Take of rhubarb t ..o ounces and a in ".'"-. I -sser cardamom Jfeds, half au ounce; brandy two pints. Digest for a week, ai:d stri. in the tincture. •Those who chuse to have a vinous tineture of rl.i.b; r'o, n.ny iufuse the above ingre- dients in a bottle of Lisbon w ine.adding to it about two ounces of proof spirits. If halt an ounce of gentian root, and a drai-hir. or Virginian snake-root be r.dded to the abo.e- ingredients, it will make the hiuer i.nctui,.- of Rhubarb. All these tinctures are designed a. stomatitis and currcborants as well as purgatives. In weakness of the stomach, indigestion, laxity of the intestines, f. xes, colicky and * A v .■■.-, ,,".(tincture of guaiacum. for donieslic use, may be made, by iryuoiVy tteo or three our.ni, V the gum in a bottle of rum or brandy. 408 APPENDIX. such like complaints, they are frequently of nit .en Ice. The dose is from halfa spoonful to three or four spoonsful or more, nccording lo the ciicuinstances of the pe- 'ient, and the purposes it is intended to answer. Paregoric Elixir.—Take of flowers of benzoin,half an ounce ; opium, two drachms. Infuse in one pound of the volatile aromatic spirit, for four or five days, frequently shaking the bottle; afterwards strain the elixir. This is an agreeabl" and safe way of administering opium. It eases pain, allays ticks ling coughs, relieves difficult breathing, and is useful in many disorders of children, par- ticularly tbe hooping-cough. The dose to an adult is from fifty to a hundred drops. Sacred F.lixir*- Take of rhubarb, cut small, ten drachms ; sticcotorinc aloes, in pow- der, six drachms; less, r cardamom seeds, half an ounce; Punch brandy, two pints. Infuse for two or three days, and then strain the elixir. This useful stomachic purge may be taken from one ounce to an ounce and a half. Stomachic Elixir.—Take of gentian root, two ounces ; Curassao oranges, one ounce; Virginian snake-root half tin ounce. Let the ingredients be bruised, and infused ibl three or four days in two pints of French brandy, afterwards strain out the elixir. 'I'his is an excellent stomachic bitter. In flatulencies, indigestion, want of appetite, and such like complaints, a small glass ofit may he taken twice a day. It likewise re- lieves the gout in the stomach, when taken in a large dose. Acid Elixir of Vitriol.— lake of the aromatic tincture, one pint, oil of vitriol, three ounces. Mix them gradually,and alter the frw. k»v«. subsided, filler the elixir thraaah paper, in a glass funnel. This is one of the best medicines which I know of for hysteric and hypochondriac pa- tients, afflicted with-flatulencies arising from relaxation or debility of tbe stomach and intestines. It will succeed where the most celebrated stomachic bitters have no effect. The dose is from ten. to forty drops, iu a glass of wine or water, or a cup of any hitter infusion, twice or thrice a-day, It should be tak. n when the stomach is most empty. Camphorated Spirit of Wine.—Dissolve an ouuce of camphor in a pint of rectified '.pirits. This, solution is chiefly employed as an embrocation in bruises, palsies, the chronic rheumatism,awl for preventing gangrenes. Theabove quantity of camphor, dissolved in half a pound of the volatile aromatic spirit, nak. s Ward's Essenre. Spirk ufMimlertrus.—Take of volatile sal ammoniac, any quantity. Pour on it gradur.Ily,disiilled vinegar, till the effervescence ceases. This medicine is useful in promoting a discharge both by the skin and urinary pas-a- ges. It is also a good external application in strains and bruises. When intended to raise a sweat, half an ounce of it in a cup of warm gruel, may be given to the patient in bed, every hour, till it lias the desired effect. VINEGARS. VINEGAR is an acid produced from vinous liquors by a second fermentation. It is an useful medicine both in inflammatory aid putrid disorders. Iu. effects are, to cool the blood, quench thiist, counteract a tendency to putrefaction, and allay inordinate mo- tions of the system. It likewise promotes the natural seen tions, ami in some cases ex- cites a copious sweat, where the warm medicines call'-dalexipharmic.tend rather to pre- vent that salutiry evacuation. Weakness, fointiugs, vomitings, and other hysteric affection*, are often relieved b* vinegar applied to the mouth and nose, or received into the stomach. It is of excel- lent use alsoin correcting many poisonous substances, when taken into the stomach ; and in promoting their expulsion by the different emunctories, when received into the blood. ot! er valuable medicines. . These effects, liowev r. are not to be expected from ..very thing that is sold under the mmeef vinegar, but from such as is sound and well prepared. The best vinegars are those prepared from French wines. It is nceesAjry for so.ur purposes that the vinegar be distilled, but as this operation re- qvi't-. >a par cu'lar chemical apparatus, we shall not insert it. Vir-;'ar ../ Litharge__Take of litharge, h It a pound ; strong vinegar, two pints. In- fus'j'.'ieiii i jetherin a moderate brat for three- days, frequently shaking the vessel; Ihn filt. r the liquor for use. # .«*_. -*< This medicine is little used, from a general notion of its being dangerous. I here vr reason, however, to believe, that the preparations of lead with vinegar are P"*"'"''; of some valuable properties, aud that they may be used in many cases with safety and A preparation ofa similar nature with the abo»e has of late been extol Id by Gou- lard, a French surgeon, as a safe and extensively useful medicine, which he calls Use Evtruet of Soforn, and orders to be made in the following manner: Tai-j of litharge, one pound: vinegar made of Freoelt wine, two pints, rat tnt» APPENDIX. 409 nsgfthcrin a glasv d earthen pipkin, and let ih»-m boil or rather simmer, for an hour, or m hour and a quarter, taking care to stir them all the while with a wooden spatula. After tne whole has stood to settle, pour olf the liquor which is upon the top into the Wtl-s for use. With this extract Goulard makes his vegeto-mineral water,'' which he recommends in a great variety of external disorders, as inflammations, burns, bruises, sprains, ulcers, Ike. He likewise pr. pares with it a number of other forms of medicine, as poultices, plas- ters, ointments, powders, &c. Vinegar of Roses.—Take of red roses, halfa pound; strong vinegar, halfa gallon. In- fuse in h close . ess. 1 for .ev. ral m*i..f •- - ~ u;;e * eat. aml Ult.a 4traul 0~~z liquor. Tbi, is principally used as tut embrocation for head achs,rkc. Vinegar of Squills.—Take of dried squills, two ounces; distilled vim-gar, two pints. Infuse-fur teri days or a fortnight in a gentle degree of heat, afterwards strain off the li- quor, add to it about a twelfth part its quantity of proof spirits. I'his medicine h.s good eifecls in disorders ofthe breast, occasioned by a load of viscid phlegm. It is also ot use in hydropic cases for promoting a discharge of urine. II.. dose is from two drachms to two ounces, according to the intention for which it is given. W lieu intended to act as a vomit, the dose ought to be large. In other esses, it must not only be exhibited in small doses, but also mixed with cinnamon '" water,or some other agreeable aromatic liquor, to prevent the nausea it might other- wise occasion. WATERS BT INFUSION, &c. Lin<e-Water.—POUR two gallons of water gradually upon a pound of fresh burnt spiiekhme; and when the ebullition ceases, stir them well together j then surfer the whole to stand at rest that the lime may settle, and afterwards filter the liquor through paper, which is to be kept in vessels closely stopt. The lime-water from calcined oyster shells ; is prepared in the same manner. Lime-water is principally used for the gravel; in which ease, from a pint to two or more ofit may be drank d.iily. Externully it is ustd for washing foul ulcers, and remo- ving the itch, and other diseases ofthe skin Compound Lima-Water.—Take sh.tviiigs of guaiacum wood, halfa pound ; liquorice root, one ounce; sassafras bark, half an ounce ; coriander seeds, three drachms; simple lime>wa'er, six pints. Infuse without heat for two days, and then strain off the liquor. In the same manner may lime-water be impregnated with the virtues of other vegeta- ble substances. Such impregnation not only n-iul. rs the water more agreeable to the palate, but also a more efficacious medicine, especially in cutaneous disorders and foul- ness of the blood and juices. It may he taken in the tame quantity as the simple water. Sublimate Water.—Dissolve eight grains of the corrosive sublimate in a pint of cinna- mon water. If a stronger solution be wanted, a double or triple quantity of sublimate may be used. The prim ip.il intention ofthis is to cleanse foul ulcers, and consume proud flesh. Styptic i't 'nter.— Take of blue vitriol and alum, each au ounce and a half; water one •int. Boil ile ,-i .ii til the salts are dissolved, then filter the liquor, and add toit a drachm ufllie oil of vitriol. 'ttiis waier is used for stopping a blet ding at the nose, and other hemorrhages; for which purpose cloths or doisilsdipt in it miht be applied to the part. 'Car Water.— Pour a gallon of water on two pounds of Norway tar, and stir them itmugly tog th .r w itli a wooden rod: after they have stood to s til. for two days, pour off the wnt.rlor use. Though tar waterfalls greatly short of the cl ...meter which has been given of it,yet it r ooisesses some medical virtues. It sensibly ra'i s the pulse, increases the secretions, and sometime s opens tin body,or occasions vomiting. A pint of it mas be dr.ink daily, or mor-, il tli stomach can be .r it. It is gent rally ordered to be tik, n on ait n.jit i sUrnach, vn. four ounres morning and evening, ""d (lie same quantity about two hours aft •. bivekinst and il inner. SIMPLE DISIIL LED WATEK'.. A ORF.AT number of .tisti.kd wateis were formerly kept in the shoos, and are Mill rei.ai.nd in some dispells-.! lories But wv con iler ihem chit-fly in the light of grate- ful diluieuts, suitable vefiiclesfor medicines of greater efficccy. or fur rend, ring disgust- ful ones more agreeable to the palate and stomach. We shall therefore insert only a t-w of those which are h st adapted to these intentions. _ Th management ofa still beinir now gruer-.lly understood, it is needless to spend time in giv in-; directions for that purpos,-. Cinnamon Water.—Steep, me pound of cinnamon bark, bruised, in a gallon and a half Of water, and one pint of brandy, for two djys; and then distil off one gallon. This is an agreeable aromatic water, possession in a high degree the fragrance and cordial-virtues ofthe spice. • See Collyrium of Lead, Mm 410 APPENDIX. Penny-royal Waters-Take of penny-royal leaves, drird, a pound and vhalf; watt* from a gallon and a half to two gallons. Draw olf hy distillation one r Ih.n. This water possesses, in a considerable d gree, the smell, lane au.1. irtin-s of the plsnt. It is given in mixtures anil juleps to hysteric patients- m . An infusion of the herb iu boiling water answers nearly the « nne purpo.ia. "» Peppermint Water.— 1 his is made in the sat. e manner as the prec ding. Spearmint Water.—This may also be prepared in the same way as the ]>cnny-royal water. Both these are useful stomachic witters ; and will sometimes relieve vomiting, espe- cially when it proceeds from indigestion, or cold viscid p'.legm. They are likewise useful in some colicky complaints,'the gout in the stomach, ike. particularly the pepper- mint-water. An infusion of the fresh plant is frequently found to have the same effect as the dis- till dw-.ter. Rose Water.—Take of roses fresh gathered,six pounds; water two gallons. Di.til off one gallon. This vvater is principally valued nn account of its fine flavour. Jamaica Pepper Water.—Take of Jamaica pepper, half a pound; water, a gallon and a half. Distil off one gallon. This is a very elegant distilled water, and may in most cases supply the place of tht more costly spice-waters. SPIRITUOUS DISTILLED WATERS. Spirituous Cinnamon W,titer.—Take of cinnamon bark, one pound ; proof spirit, and common water, of each eue gallon. Steep the cinnamon in the liquor for two days; then distil olf one gallon Spirituous Jamaica Pepper Water.—Take of Jamaica pepper, half a pound : proof spirit, three gallons : water, two gallons. Distil olf three gallons. This is a sufficiently agreeable cordial, and may supply the place ofthe Aromatio mater. WHETS. Alum Wlicy.—Boil two drachms of powdered alum in a pint of milk till it it curdled -, slien strain out the whey. This whey is beneficial in an immoderate- flow ofthe menses, and in a diabetes, ores- eessive discharge of urine. The dose is two. three or four ounces, recording as the stomach will bear it, threa limes a day. If it sliould occasion vomiting, it may be diluted. Mustard Whey.—Take milk and water of each a pint; bruised mustard seed, an cure* and a half. Boil them together till the curd is perfectly separated; afterwards strain the whey through a cloth. This is the most elegant, and by no means the least effieacinus method of exhibiting mustard. It warms and invigorates the habit, and promotes the different secretion.. Hence, in the low state of nervous fevers, it will often supply the place of wine. It ii also of use in the chronic rheumatism, palsy, dropsy, Ike. The addition ofa little sugar will render it more agreeable. The dose is an ordinary tea-cupful four times a-day. Scorbutic Whey.—This whey is made by boiling halfa pint of the scorbutic juices ia a quart of cow's milk. More benefit, however, 11 to be expected from eating the planti, khan from their expressed juices. The scorbutic plants are, bitter oranges, brook]ime, gaiden scurvy-grass, and watt. •cesses. A number of other wheys maybe prepared nearly in the same manner, as orange whey, cream of tartar whey, &c. These are cooling pleasant drinks in feven, and may he rendered cordial, when necessary, by the addition of wine. WINES. THE effects of wine are to raise the pulse, promote perspiration, warm the habit, and •xhilirate the spirits. The red wines besides these effects, have an astringent quality, by which they strengthen the lone of the stomach and intestines,and by this means pro.e serviceable in restraining immoderate secretions. The thin sharp wines have a different tendency. They pass off freely by the differ. ent emunctories, and gently open the body. The effects of the full bodied wines are, however, much more durable than those of the thinner. All sweet wines contain a glutinous substance, and do not pass of freely. Hence thi j will heat the body more than an equal quantity of any other wine, though it should con- tain fully as much spirit. From the obvious qualities of wine, it must appear to be an excellent cordial rncdi- •in.-. Indeed, to say the truth, it is worth all the rest put together. But to answer this character, it must be sound mid good. No b-nefit is to b- espeet- •d from the common trash that is often sold hy the name of wine, without poiirssiiiK one drop of the juice of the grape. Perhaps no medicine is more rurcl) obtained genuine than wine. Wine is not only used as a medicine, but is also employed as a menstruum for extract- ing tho virtne* of other medifliual substances; for which it is not ill adapted, being • APPENDIX. ill soaipeund of water, inflammable spirit, and acid ; hy which means it is enabled to act •H ■■. ■ regita' '-and ..inn nl substances and also to dissolve some bodies of the metallic kind sos. i.'. unprigi ut- it. If with their virtues, as steel, antimony. Ike. w^ttthrln inn, Wine.—Take of rhubarb, half an ounce ; w orm-seed, an ounce. Bruise Wein, aid infuse without heat in two pints of red port wine for a few days, then strain off lb wine. As the stomachs of persons afflicted with worms are always debilitated, red wine alone will ofteiipron-serviceable: It must, however, have still better eftects when joined with bitter and purgative it).;r«ili tils,as in tie above form. A glass of this wine may be taken tw ice or thrice a-day. Antimotrial Wi. c— Take a glass of antimony, reduced to a fine powder, half an ounce. Lisbon wine, eight ounces. Dig- st. without heat, for three or four days, now and then shaking the bottl.-; nfn rwards filter the wine through paper. The dose of ihis wine varies according to tin- intention. As an alterative and dia- pliorrtic, it may betaknfrom ten to fifty or sixty drops. In a large dose it generally pmvi* cathartic, or. \cit. . vomiting. Bitter Wine.— Take of g. nti.«i root.yellow rind of lemon-peel, fresh each one ounce ; Innu pepper, (wo ilrach. s; mountain wine, two pints. Infuse without heat for a week, awl strain out the wine for use. Iu com pi* in ts arising from weakness ofthe stomach, or indigestion, a glass of this wine may b. taken an lioilr before dinner and supper. Ipecacuanha Wine.— lake of ipi cucuanha, in powder, one ounce ; mountain wine, a pint. Infusi for three or four days; then filter the tincture. This is a safe vomit, mid answi rs extremely well for such persons as cannot swallow thi ;,uv\iUr. or whose stomachs are too irritable to bear it. I Ik dose is from one ouuce to an ounce and a half. tiuilybrate or Steel Wine.—Take filings of iron, two ounces; cinnamon and mace, of each two drachms ; Rhenish wine, two pints. Infuse for three or lour weeks, frequent- ly shaking the bottle ; then pass the wine through a filter. In obstructions of the menses, this preparation of iron may be taken, in the dose of halfa wine-glass twice or thrice a-day. The medicine would probably be as good if made with Lisbon wine, sharpened with hn II .in on nee of tbe cream of tartar, or a small quantity of the vitriolic acid. Sioinaeh Wine—Take of Peruvian bark, grossly powdered, an ounce ; cardamom weii,,. id orange-peel, bruised, of each two drachms. Infuse in a bottle of white port •r l.i I. 1 wine, for live or six days ; then strain off the wine. I'ii vine is not only of service in debility of the stomach and intestines, but may al- so be taken as a preventive, by persons liable to the intermittent fever, or who reside ia places where this disease prevails. It will be of use likewise to those who recover slowly after fevers of any kind, as it assists digestion; and helps to restore the tone and trigoui-of the system. A glass of it may be taken two or three times a-day. A GLOSSARY. ALTHOUGH terms of art have been sedulously avoided in the composition of this treatise, it is impossible entirely to banish technical phrases when writing on medicine, a science that has been less generally attended toby mankind, and continues therefore tob- more infected with the jargon of the schools, than perhaps any other. Several per- sons having expressed their opinion that a Glossary would make this work more general- ly intelligible, the following concise explanation of tht few terms of art that occur, has been added in compliance with their sentiments, and to fulfil the original intention of this treatise, by rendering it intelligible and useful to all ranks and classes cf mankind. iBl'OMEN, The belly. ZlUC, the flowers of, popular remedy for the epilepsy, 264. FINIS, ---- In his article on " The Ocean | Steamer," in the July Harper, Mr. Jacob i Abbott gives an explanation of sea-sickness, | which is both scientific and likely to upset popular notions on the subject. The sickness is not owing to the effect of the rhotionof the vessel upon the digestive organs, but, prinia- rily, to an affection of the brain^ the nausea being secondary and symptomatic. While we *>re on the land, and surrounded by objects that are near and fixed, the brain maintains its " instinct of equilibrium,"—as that instinct is called by which the brain takes cognizance of the relation of the body to the perpendicu- lar. "When we are on the sea, where, no fixed objects are in view, the s^nsjajjpptTUi- librium is lost) efforts to retain it^aspace gid- diness, the liver and digestive (Wgans are in turn affected, and' sickness results. The same sensation is often experienced by pas- sengers in a close stage coach, cut off, not from fresh air as is commonly supposed, but from a sight of the stable objects of nature. If voyagers over rough and rolling billows had in near view any uomovina objects on which to fix the eye, and by yKhwp to satisfy the instiDCt referred to, the discomforts ' sea-sickness would be avoided. /^>/0 I"- CONTENTS.
